1:
The witnesses treat this opening verse as the gateway to the only miracle recorded in all four Gospels (so the grammatical-historical commentators and Matthew Henry alike note), and they are unanimous about the care with which the scene is set: Jesus withdraws across the Sea of Galilee — which John alone also calls the Sea of Tiberias, after Herod’s new capital — while a great crowd follows for his healings, and the Passover draws near (v.4).

The grammatical-historical voices are busiest, and largely agree. With one mind they read “after these things” as an indefinite note of time, not immediate sequence; Robertson and Calvin reckon nearly a year separates the Jerusalem of chapter 5 from this Galilean Passover, and they harmonise the scene with Mark 6:30–44, Matthew 14:13–21, and Luke 9:10–17. They explain the lake’s several names, locate the desert place near the eastern Bethsaida (Robertson, Clarke), and observe that John, writing to supplement the others, records few miracles because his aim is the discourses (Barnes, Calvin).

Reading the same event devotionally, the Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry dwell on the crowd’s self-forgetful hunger to hear Christ — a rebuke, Calvin says, to our sloth, and a comfort that Christ “provides food for them before they have asked it.” Henry adds that Christ lived “continually in a crowd,” welcomed the poor multitude, and questioned Philip only to prove him, “for he himself knew what he would do” (v.6).

The Latin fathers supply the deepest theology and the allegory. Augustine’s reflection makes the miracle a window on providence: the feeding is a smaller wonder than God’s daily creating of harvest “from a few grains,” but it astonishes because it is rare — “He therefore created as God creates.” Hilary marvels that the bread multiplied unseen in the breakers’ hands. Then Augustine, Bede, and Alcuin read the signs figuratively: the five barley loaves are the five books of the Law, coarse and husk-bound, made many only when Christ “breaks” and opens them; the two fishes are the priestly and royal offices, or the prophets and the psalms; the twelve baskets are the apostles, who carry the deeper truths the multitude cannot hold.

The chief debate is one of harmony. Since the Synoptics have the disciples first urge Jesus to send the crowd away, while John has Jesus open the matter with Philip, Augustine reconciles the accounts (Jesus asked Philip later, an order John alone preserves), whereas Chrysostom will allow they may simply be “two different occasions.” The Eastern Chrysostom also draws the morals — withdraw from strife as Christ did, and give thanks at every meal as he did here — while Theophylact answers the Manichees from the multiplied bread: a good God would not multiply an evil thing.
2:
Here the witnesses are few and their interest narrowly grammatical, but they fill in the scene with care. The grammatical-historical commentators carry the verse. Ellicott and Robertson press the imperfect tenses — better “were following … were beholding … was doing” — so that the picture is not of a single sighting but of a crowd that “kept on following” while Jesus “kept on healing the sick”; Robertson glosses the sick as the strengthless, tous asthenountas, and notes they had been “beholding the signs… for a long time” (John 2:23), most of which John never records. Robertson’s descriptive imperfect “pictures the crowd” streaming after Jesus, though John omits the detail of the boat for Christ and “the rapid race of the crowd on foot.” Ellicott explains the swelling throng from three converging causes: the Baptist had just been killed and his followers now attached themselves to Jesus, the Twelve had returned from their mission, and Passover pilgrims were already streaming toward Jerusalem along the eastern caravan-road, so that “the throng would increase as He went.” Gill, Barnes, and Clarke add only that the multitude came from the Galilean towns, drawn by the signs (Barnes cross-refers v.26 and Matthew 14:14).

Calvin alone reads it pastorally, and with a warning, in the Reformed key. The people’s eagerness, he grants, was real — they forgot themselves to follow Christ into a desert place, and this “shames our own sloth” toward “the heavenly doctrine,” for “very rarely does it happen that Christ finds us free and disengaged from the entanglements of the world.” He adds the comfort that Christ “of his own accord takes care of those who neglect themselves in order to follow him,” providing food “before they have asked it”; and though he sometimes lets the godly “be exhausted and almost fainting with hunger,” from heaven “he beholds our wants, and is careful to relieve them, as far as is necessary for our welfare.” Yet beneath their zeal Calvin marks the flaw: their ardour “arose from this, that, having beheld his power in miracles, they were convinced that he was some great prophet.” A following built on wonders rather than on the word will not last — the very judgment the chapter’s later verses confirm.
3:
The comment here is thin and chiefly topographical, yet the witnesses make the most of the mountain. The grammatical-historical voices fix the place: Gill sets it “in a desert place near Bethsaida,” and Clarke explains that Jesus “withdrew to this place for a little rest,” since he and his disciples “had been so thronged with the multitudes… that they had not time to take necessary food” (Mark 6:31). Ellicott reads “the mountain,” the hill-country on the eastern shore, and Robertson pictures Jesus climbing “from the level of the Jordan valley up into the high hill on the eastern side” to sit — the imperfect ekathēto, “was sitting, a picture of repose” — noting that Mark 6:46 and Matthew 14:23 tell of a further ascent to pray after the miracle. Wesley adds only that he went up “before the people overtook him.”

Calvin, in the Reformed manner, turns the retreat into a doctrine of providence. Christ as man genuinely “sought a place of retirement,” forming that purpose “as man”; “but the purpose of God was different, which he willingly obeyed.” So although “he avoided the sight of men, yet he permits himself to be led by the hand of God as into a crowded theater” — there gathering “a larger assembly of men than in any populous city,” and winning “greater celebrity… than if it had happened in the open market-place.” The lesson Calvin draws is for us: “we are therefore taught by this example to form our plans in conformity to the course of events,” yet “in such a manner that, if the result be different from what we expected, we may not be displeased that God is above us, and regulates everything according to his pleasure.” The quiet ascent of the mountain thus becomes a small parable of submission to a providence that overrules even our desire for rest.

The witnesses also note that this is the same mountain to which Christ will withdraw alone after the miracle (v.15): the chapter opens and the feeding closes upon the same height, so that the day is framed between two climbs of one hill — the ascent that now seeks rest will then seek escape and prayer. Clarke makes the link explicit, identifying the mountain of v.15 with “that on which he was with his disciples previously” here. Even the slight notice thus sets the stage on which the whole sign will be performed and interpreted.
4:
All the witnesses here are grammatical-historical, and they read the calendar against the festival. John’s note that “the Passover, a feast of the Jews, was nigh” is, Barnes observes, one of the explanatory asides that show he “wrote for those who were unacquainted with Jewish customs.” Gill, Lightfoot, and Clarke supply the rabbinic background in detail: the feast was discussed thirty days beforehand, and from the fifteenth of Adar the nation set about its public business — Lightfoot lists how “they repaired the ways, the streets, the bridges, the pools,” painted the sepulchres, “burnt the red heifer,” “bored the ear of the Hebrew servant,” and “cleansed the lepers,” so that the festival was already “in the eyes and mouth of this people,” and especially in the last fifteen days. Most reckon this the third Passover of Christ’s ministry (Gill, Robertson); Clarke, following Calmet, places the scene “about ten or twelve days before” it. Gill is uncertain whether Christ went up to this feast at all, and Robertson is sure he did not — linking the absence to the hostility of John 7:1, where Jesus “failed to go… because of the hostility in Jerusalem.” Robertson notes John’s fondness for such notes of time, the adverb “near” (engys) recurring at John 2:13 and John 11:55.

Ellicott alone lifts the note above chronology. The article matters — “the feast” — and the detail is “not simply a note of time, but gives a key of interpretation to the sign itself, and to the discourse which followed.” The bread that Christ multiplies and the Bread-of-Life sermon that interprets it are both to be heard against the Passover, with its memory of the manna and its looking for redemption; that festival frame, more than the bare date, is why John records it at all (compare the unnamed feast of John 5:1). What the others read as a calendar marker, Ellicott reads as the hermeneutical setting of the whole chapter.

The note also looks ahead. The Passover recalled the exodus and the manna of the wilderness — the very bread the crowd will soon throw up against Christ (vv.31–32). Ellicott’s “key of interpretation” is precisely this: the sign of bread is given in the season when Israel remembered God’s bread from heaven, so that the festival silently poses the question the discourse will answer. What the others read as a date, then, the chapter uses as a frame, and the manna-memory of the feast becomes the foil for the true bread that came down from heaven.
5:
The exchange with Philip is, as Ellicott and Robertson stress, peculiar to John, told from inside the circle around Jesus. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the harmony, the geography, and the grammar. Gill pictures Christ “engaged in close conversation with his disciples” before lifting up his eyes; Robertson marks the same gesture (“lifting up his eyes,” as at John 4:35; 11:41; 17:1) as Jesus “looked down from the mountain on the approaching multitude.” Where the Synoptics have the disciples urge Jesus to dismiss the crowd, John has Jesus himself open the question — “Whence are we to buy bread?”, a deliberative subjunctive (Robertson). Ellicott insists the accounts are “different from, but not opposed to” one another: John does not deny the disciples spoke first, and writes from the vantage of those nearest the Lord, picturing “the first-called Andrew and Peter, James and John, and Philip… closer to Him than the others.” Several note Jesus turned to Philip because, as Wesley guesses, he “had the care of providing victuals for the family of the apostles,” and because Philip was of the nearby Bethsaida and “better acquainted with the country” (Clarke, who prefers the reading “may we buy”). Robertson hears behind the whole scene the old wonderings of Numbers 11:13 (Moses) and 2 Kings 4:42 (Elisha).

On why Philip in particular, Ellicott reads a deliberate test of character (resolved in v.6): the disciple had seen Cana and the other signs, but had he grasped the principle of divine sufficiency behind them? “There is something in the character of Philip which this occasion may test.” Calvin, in the Reformed key, harmonises the singular and plural differently — Jesus “spoke according to the opinion entertained by all,” so the word to Philip is a word to the sleeping minds of all the disciples, meant to “arouse” them, “so that they may, at least, have their eyes open to behold what shall be immediately exhibited to them.”

The witnesses agree, too, that the question is the first move in a deliberate sequence. Having drawn the disciples’ inability into the open through Philip, Christ will let Andrew’s smaller arithmetic follow (vv.8–9) before he acts, so that the confession of helplessness is complete before the miracle answers it. The lifting of the eyes, Robertson notes, is the same gesture with which Jesus elsewhere turns from the seen to the unseen harvest; here it gathers the multitude into view precisely so that their need may become the occasion of a sign no human provision could meet.
6:
Thin but pointed, and entirely in the grammatical-historical register, this verse turns on the evangelist’s aside — “this he said to prove him, for he himself knew what he would do.” The commentators agree it is John’s own explanatory comment; Clarke notes that John “often interweaves his own judgment with the facts he relates, which St. Matthew rarely ever does,” and adds that the other evangelists report Christ teaching and healing “till it was near the close of the day” before the feeding (Matthew 14:14; Mark 6:34; Luke 9:11). Robertson is careful with the verb: peirazōn here is “testing him, not… tempting,” the good sense (unlike Matthew 4:1), and the imperfect “what he would do” is John explaining the motive just as at John 2:25. Gill agrees the proving was “tempting him, trying his faith… not as ignorant of it himself,” but in order “to discover it to him,” to prepare the disciples for the miracle, and to make it “appear the more illustrious and marvellous.” Barnes sums it simply: “to try him; to see if he had faith.”

Ellicott turns the verse into a window on Christ’s pedagogy. “There is for Him no difficulty. He of Himself knows what He is about to do.” The question is an examination. Philip had been present at Cana and had seen the wine multiplied; “other signs had spoken to the eye, and a fuller teaching had spoken to the ear. How far had either spoken to the spirit?” He had felt the divine presence in separate instances, but had he realised it “as a law of life, holding for every need that could arise”? The student “has learnt individual facts, but has he laid hold of the principle which underlies them?” Teaching from without “depends upon the teacher”; the lesson grasped from within “is the true education of the man himself.” So Christ tests Philip not to inform himself but to bring the disciple to self-knowledge — “He has been taught; he is now to be examined.”

The verse, brief as it is, thus carries the chapter’s recurring theme: that Christ’s questions are never for his own information but for ours. As at the tomb of Lazarus and before the man born blind, he asks in order to draw faith into the open and expose the heart’s real reckonings. Here the proving of Philip becomes the proving of every disciple who, set before an impossible need, must choose between counting the visible means and trusting the One who “himself knew what he would do.”
7:
Philip’s reply — two hundred pennyworth of bread “is not sufficient” — draws the grammatical-historical voices to the money and its meaning. A denarius (about sixteen and two-thirds cents, Robertson reckons) was a labourer’s daily wage (Matthew 20:2), so two hundred of them is figured at roughly six pounds in the commentators’ currency — more, Clarke notes, “than our Lord and all his disciples were worth of this world’s goods.” Ellicott presses the point further: the sum is “in actual labour-value nearly a workman’s yearly wage,” and he recalls that in A.D. 14 the mutinous Pannonian legions demanded a denarius a day as decent pay (Tacitus). For Philip “this large sum seems an impossibility”; “he states the difficulty, and leaves it.” Robertson observes that the figure of two hundred denarii is shared with Mark 6:37, while the clause “that every one may take a little” is John’s own detail. Calvin’s long note on the old French coinage reaches the same end: probably Philip “thought that there was a greater number of people present,” and, the disciples being “poor and ill supplied with money,” he “intended to alarm Christ by the greatness of the sum,” meaning that they were “not wealthy enough to entertain so many people.”

Where the witnesses differ is only in tone, and in how much blame to assign. Gill reads the answer as spoken “very quick and short, and in a carnal and unbelieving way.” Ellicott agrees that Philip “has not really learnt the lessons of the earlier teaching”: the question “does not suggest to him the true answer of divine sufficiency, but leads him to think of the human difficulty.” The verse thus completes the test set in v.6 — faced with the need, the disciple who had seen Cana reckons only the human arithmetic, and so fails the examination by counting pence where he might have trusted power.

The arithmetic itself is part of the lesson the witnesses draw. Two hundred denarii would not give each of five thousand so much as “a little,” and that shortfall is the point: the disciple’s careful sum proves only how far the need outruns every human supply. So the verse leaves the reader, with Philip, at the end of calculation and the beginning of faith — the place where, the commentators agree, the miracle must take over, and where the chapter means us to feel our own poverty before the bread of life is named.
8:
A brief, identifying note, handled by the grammatical-historical commentators, whose whole interest is in Andrew, “Simon Peter’s brother.” Ellicott and Robertson place him firmly within the inner circle around Jesus. He was, Ellicott recalls, “one of the two disciples of the Baptist who first followed Jesus,” and John’s own companion at that first meeting (John 1:40); he is “always named as one of the first group of the Twelve” (compare Matthew 10:2) and was “in some way… specially connected with Philip” (John 1:44), the two being named together here, again at John 12:22, and in the apostolic lists of Mark 3:18 and Acts 1:13. Robertson notes the idiom “one of” (without the partitive, as at John 13:23) and dwells on Andrew’s abiding distinction: it “was precisely this that he brought Simon to Christ” — so that even here John identifies him by his relation to the more famous brother he had won. Robertson adds the curious tradition of the Muratorian Fragment, that “Andrew received the revelation for John to write the Fourth Gospel.”

The one harmonising note is Clarke’s: “the other evangelists attribute this answer to the apostles in general.” So here, as so often in this chapter, John supplies the individual detail — the named disciple, his exact words — where the Synoptics keep the remark general. The effect, the witnesses imply, is to set Andrew once more in his characteristic role: not the boldest of the Twelve, but the one who notices the little there is (the lad, the loaves) and brings it to Jesus, leaving the impossibility for the Lord to resolve.

Gill adds that Andrew, like Philip, was “of Bethsaida,” and that he speaks here having “heard what Christ said to Philip, and what answer he returned” — drawn into the conversation by the same need. The portrait the witnesses build is consistent with the rest of the Gospel: Andrew appears three times in John, and each time he is bringing someone or something to Jesus — his brother Simon (John 1:40), the lad with the loaves here, and the inquiring Greeks (John 12:22). His instinct is to gather the little that is at hand and lay it before the Lord, even while confessing it is nothing among so many — the disposition the miracle is about to honour.
9:
The grammatical-historical commentators savour the eye-witness detail John alone supplies — the lad, the five barley loaves, the two fishes — and dwell on the meanness of the fare. Gill wonders whether the lad “belonged to Christ and his disciples,” carrying their provisions (which “shows how meanly Christ and his disciples lived”), or had simply come “to sell what he had got.” All press that barley was the inferior grain. Clarke notes it “scarcely bore one-third of the value of wheat in the east,” the food of the poor and a token of meanness (he cites Revelation 6:6, the handfuls of barley of Ezekiel 13:19, and Plutarch’s Artaxerxes reduced to barley bread), so that the provision exemplifies “the self-denying doctrine preached by Christ and his apostles” in their own manner of living. The word for “fishes” (opsaria) they note is a diminutive meaning the small relish eaten with bread (Ellicott, Robertson), and the “lad” (paidarion, only here in the New Testament) is a little boy or slave who had followed the crowd, perhaps hoping to find a purchaser. Ellicott catches the rhetorical force of the diminutives: where Philip had dwelt on “the greatness of the need,” Andrew’s whole remark “rests upon the smallness of their power to help” — “but what are these among so many?”

Lightfoot supplies the telling parallel, comparing 2 Kings 4:42: the rabbis enlarged Elisha’s miracle so that “every hundred men had their single loaf set before them,” twenty loaves with the first-fruits and the green ear feeding two thousand two hundred. “By the same proportion,” Lightfoot observes, in Christ’s feeding “one single loaf must serve for a thousand” — the greater Prophet outdoing the lesser, as the discourse to come will press home. The scantiness, then, is not incidental but pointed: the smaller the means, the clearer the power that multiplies them.

Robertson and Ellicott both stress that the diminutives are not incidental but rhetorical: paidarion, a mere lad; krithinous, barley, “considered an inferior sort of bread”; opsaria, two little relish-fish. Everything in Andrew’s inventory is small, and deliberately so, that the contrast with the five thousand may be felt. The witnesses thus read the verse as the last word of human insufficiency before the divine sufficiency answers it — the smaller the loaves and the meaner the fare, the plainer it will be that the abundance to come is Christ’s and not the lad’s.
10:
The verse describes only the seating of the crowd, yet the witnesses divide along familiar lines. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the realia. Gill notes Christ did not reprove the disciples’ unbelief but simply “ordered them directly to place the people upon the ground, and seat them in rows by hundreds and by fifties.” Robertson glosses “sit down” as anapesein, “fall back, lie down, recline,” and lingers on the “much grass” (chortos polus) — green spring grass on the hillside (the season fixed by the Passover of v.4), the same word used of hay in 1 Corinthians 3:12; the people were arranged, he adds, “in orderly groups by hundreds and fifties,” like garden-beds (prasiai, Mark 6:40). Clarke takes the “much grass” (Vulgate faenum) as a further mark that the Passover was near, since “in Palestine the grass is ready for mowing in March.” Ellicott observes that John’s “much grass” is an addition, matching Mark’s “green grass,” and that the word for “men” (andres) means males as such, so that the five thousand is “besides the women and children” (Matthew 14:21).

Calvin, for the Reformed, turns the scene into a lesson in faith. He blames the disciples’ earlier slowness to “cherish the hope which their Master held out,” yet gives “no small praise” to their “cheerful obedience in now complying with his injunction, though they know not what is his intention.” The crowd shows the same readiness, sitting down “as soon as a single word of command has been pronounced,” while “uncertain about the result.” This, Calvin says, “is the trial of true faith, when God commands men to walk, as it were, in darkness.” The lesson is plain: “let us learn not to be wise in ourselves, but, amidst great confusion, still to hope for a prosperous issue, when we follow the guidance of God, who never disappoints his own people.”

The witnesses also catch the quiet order of the scene. The people are seated “in ranks” before a single loaf appears, which Chrysostom (at v.1) read as teaching that with Christ “things that are not are as things that are.” Robertson’s garden-bed image (prasiai) and Gill’s rows of hundreds and fifties picture a multitude marshalled in calm expectation, so that the miracle is performed not in confusion but in the ordered hush of obedience — the very faith Calvin praises, sitting down at a word with nothing yet in view.
11:
At the heart of the miracle the witnesses concentrate on two things: the giving of thanks, and the abundance. The grammatical-historical commentators note the grammar — Jesus “having given thanks” (eucharistēsas, the usual grace before meat, Deuteronomy 8:10), where the Synoptics say “blessed”; he then “distributed” (diedōken, to give to several) to those reclining “as much as they would” (hoson ēthelon, Robertson). Clarke fixes the proportion — with five loaves and five thousand men, “there was one loaf to every thousand,” besides the women and children. Gill notes simply that Christ took the loaves and fishes “into his hands… in order to feed the multitude with them.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the verse’s fullest theology. From Christ’s thanksgiving he teaches that “whenever we take food, we ought to begin with prayer,” for our food is an evidence of God’s “infinite goodness and fatherly love,” and to “swallow them down without thinking of God” is to be “guilty of sacrilege, and of profaning the gifts of God” (1 Timothy 4:4). The miracle confirms Matthew 6:33 — if Christ cared for those “led to him only by a sudden impulse,” how should he desert those who seek him steadily? — and shows that the Father appointed him, “even in his human nature,” as the channel through whom all blessings come (Ephesians 5:20). Yet, Calvin adds, he provided no delicacies but “barley-bread and fish without sauce,” and still feeds the whole world by the unseen miracle of nature, teaching that man lives “by the word which proceedeth out of the mouth of God” (Deuteronomy 8:3).

Cyril of Jerusalem stands apart, reading the verse through the Eucharist. Hearing Christ’s thanksgiving and breaking of bread, he recalls the institution — “Take, eat, this is My Body… this is My Blood” — and presses that, since Christ himself so declared, none should “dare to doubt any longer.” It is the one sacramental voice here, anticipating the Bread-of-Life discourse to come.

The witnesses note, finally, the manner of distribution: Christ gives to the disciples, and the disciples to the people, so that the Twelve become, as Henry put it at v.1, “the servitors at Christ’s table.” The bread grows, Calvin observes, “among their hands,” teaching “that God blesses our labour when we are serviceable to each other.” Thus even the mechanics of the miracle preach: the abundance is Christ’s alone, but it reaches the multitude through the ministry of those who carry what he has blessed.
12:
The gathering of the fragments draws a single, near-unanimous lesson, with the grammatical-historical commentators leading. Robertson clarifies that the “broken pieces” (klasmata) are not crumbs from the ground but the pieces broken by Christ (Mark 6:41) and not consumed; the purpose clause “that nothing be lost” is found only in John. Ellicott notes that John, characteristically, connects with Christ himself what the other evangelists relate of the disciples — “it is from this passage only that we know that the gathering of the fragments followed His express command.” From this all draw frugality. Barnes presses it at length: Christ, “though he had an infinite supply at his disposal,” taught “that the bounties of Providence are not to be squandered.” The rich especially should remember that “what they have is the gift of God… not given them to waste, nor to spend in riot,” but “to sustain life, to excite gratitude, to fit for the active service of God” (he cites Nehemiah 9:25 and Nehemiah 8:10).

The Jewish commentators add the local custom. Lightfoot and Clarke note that diners left a portion — the peah — for those who served (Lightfoot tells of R. Joshua, who “ate all up, and left nothing to her that ministered”), and Clarke quotes the rabbinic warning that “great will be the punishment of those who waste the crumbs of food.” Lightfoot observes, without quite identifying the custom with the miracle, that “the twelve baskets full of fragments… answered to the number of the twelve apostles that ministered.” The witnesses are thus of one mind: the lavishness of the miracle is no warrant for waste; the very abundance is gathered up, counted, and accounted for.

The witnesses press, too, the spiritual extension of the command. Baxter, whom Henry quotes, draws the greater lesson: “how much less should we lose God’s word, or helps, or our time, or such greater mercies!” If the bread that Christ multiplied was not to be wasted, still less the truth he breaks and gives. So the gathering of the fragments becomes a parable of stewardship over every gift, and the same hand that fills with abundance teaches that abundance is held in trust, to be gathered, kept, and put to the use of which God approves.
13:
A brief verse on the twelve baskets, carried by the grammatical-historical voices with one devotional addition. Gill pictures the disciples gathering “the several broken bits of bread, which lay about upon the grass, which the people had left.” Robertson notes the baskets are kophinoi, the stout wicker baskets (Wycliffe’s “coffins”) used by all four Gospels here, in deliberate distinction from the soft, frail spyrides used at the feeding of the four thousand (Mark 8:8; Matthew 15:37) — a distinction Jesus himself preserves when he recalls both miracles (Mark 8:19–20; Matthew 16:9–10). He observes the obvious symmetry — “one for each of the apostles” — and asks, almost playfully, “what about the lad?” Ellicott simply refers the reader to the parallel at Matthew 14:20.

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the abundance. At the feeding of the four thousand the baskets equalled the loaves (Matthew 15:37); so here, where a smaller supply feeds a greater crowd yet leaves nearly double over, “we see more clearly of what value is that blessing of God, against the sight of which we deliberately shut our eyes.” But he at once repeats the verse’s restraint: Christ commands the gathering “for illustrating the miracle,” yet thereby “exhorts his disciples to frugality,” since “the increase of the bounty of God ought not to be an excitement to luxury.” Those who “have abundance,” Calvin warns, “will one day render an account of their immoderate wealth, if they do not carefully and faithfully apply their superfluity to purposes which are good, and of which God approves.”

The witnesses read the twelve baskets, finally, as the miracle’s standing proof. The remnant is greater than the original supply — five loaves becoming twelve baskets full — so that the very leftovers testify against any thought of delusion: real bread was eaten, and more remained than was begun with. Henry notes that the twelve baskets, “one for each disciple,” repaid the Twelve “with interest” for their willingness to part with the little they had. Thus the close of the feeding gathers up its whole lesson: with Christ the supply never merely meets the need but overflows it, “bread enough, and to spare, in our Father’s house.”
14:
The crowd’s verdict — “This is of a truth that prophet that should come into the world” — gathers the witnesses around its messianic expectation and its inadequacy. The grammatical-historical commentators identify “that prophet” with the Messiah foretold in Deuteronomy 18:15 (Barnes and Clarke, with Genesis 49:10), the power and benevolence of the miracle showing him to be “the long-expected Messiah.” Robertson reads the oldest text as “signs” (plural), this one “added to those already wrought” (v.2; cf. John 2:23), and parses the inchoative imperfect “they began to say.” He marks the crowd “on the tiptoe of expectation,” believing Jesus “the political Messiah of Pharisaic hope” — “the prophet that cometh,” a phrase peculiar to John but with the same idea in Acts (Acts 3:22; Acts 7:37) — the very figure for whom they had once taken the Baptist (John 1:21; Ellicott). Clarke exclaims how “near… to the kingdom of heaven” these people were. Ellicott also catches a smaller point: the lectionary habit of adding “that Jesus did” at the head of a church reading shows how the verse was used in worship, while “the reception or rejection of Christ is always present to” John’s thoughts.

Yet the deeper witnesses mark the flaw in the confession. Henry, in the Puritan vein, grants that “even the common Jews… knew most of Him who is the end of the law,” more than the despising Pharisees; but he warns that men “may acknowledge Christ as that Prophet, and still turn a deaf ear to him,” for there is in “the corrupt unsanctified soul” such “incoherence” that one may own him “and yet… not continue in” the doctrine. Calvin, for the Reformed, agrees the miracle bore some fruit in their owning him as Messiah — “Christ had no other object in view” — but warns that “immediately they apply to a different and improper purpose the knowledge which they have obtained.” It is, he says, “a fault extremely common among men, to corrupt and pervert his truth by their falsehoods, as soon as he has revealed himself”; and so even those who “appear to have entered into the right path… immediately fall away” — a perversion the attempt to crown him by force (v.15) will at once expose.
15:
The witnesses are unanimous that the crowd, fed and dazzled, tried to seize Jesus and “make him a king by force,” and that he answered by withdrawing alone to the mountain; they divide only in emphasis. The Puritan Matthew Henry anatomises the crowd’s “irregular zeal”: it rested on a false notion of Christ’s kingdom as worldly, was “excited by the love of the flesh” (a king who feeds without their toil), and aimed at “a secular design” — shaking off the Roman yoke. Christ’s flight he reads as humility, a testimony “against ambition” and “against faction and sedition”; those “who force honours upon Christ which he has not required… do him the greatest dishonour.”

The grammatical-historical commentators say the same in historical terms. The people held the Messiah to be a temporal prince (Barnes, Ellicott), and Robertson reads “a movement to start a revolution against Roman rule… proclaiming Jesus King and driving away Pilate”; the crisis called for quick action, so Jesus hurried off even the disciples — “sympathetic with the revolutionary impulse” — and went up to pray (Mark 6:46; Matthew 14:23). Clarke’s lesson: “no saving change can be brought about by any might or any power, but by the Spirit of the Lord of hosts” (Zechariah 4:6).

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses a sharper point: there was “some ground” for wishing to honour Christ as king, but the people “erred egregiously in taking upon themselves the liberty of” appointing him, for Scripture makes that God’s own prerogative — “I have appointed my king on my holy hill of Zion” (Psalm 2:6) — and the kingship they contrived was earthly, “utterly inconsistent with his person.”

The Latin fathers add the paradox that becomes the verse’s deepest note. He who fled from being made a king was a king already (Augustine) — “not made king by men… but making men kings,” whose kingdom the prophets foretold and which is manifested after the judgment; and his flight no more contradicts his going up to pray than escape contradicts prayer, “for whenever escape is necessary, there is great necessity for prayer.” The Eastern Chrysostom is blunter — “See what the belly can do”: their zeal for the Sabbath forgotten now their stomachs are filled, they would enthrone the very Christ who flees worldly dignity to teach us to despise it.
16:
The narrative turns to the disciples at evening, and the witnesses read the hour two ways. The grammatical-historical Robertson fixes the time precisely — “the late hour… real evening,” not the mid-afternoon “evening” of Matthew 14:15 — and notes the disciples “were in no hurry to start back,” their destination being Capernaum in John, the western Bethsaida in Mark 6:45. Gill marks it “the last of the evenings,” the first having fallen before the miracle. Barnes and Wesley simply refer the storm and the walking on the sea to the fuller Synoptic accounts (Matthew 14:23; Mark 6:47).

The Eastern Chrysostom reads the hour devotionally, as a measure of love, and at length. Christ, he says, “provideth for the good of his disciples not only when He is present in the body, but also when far away.” The disciples “waited for Him until evening, expecting that He would come unto them; but when even was come, they could no longer endure not to seek their Master; so great a love possessed them.” They did not plead “it is now evening, and night hath overtaken us… the place is dangerous, the time unsafe,” but, “goaded by their longing,” entered the ship — and “it is not without a cause that the Evangelist hath declared the time also, but by it to show the warmth of their love.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, supplies the strategy behind the separation. Christ “intended to conceal himself until the crowd should disperse,” knowing “how difficult it is to allay a popular tumult”; had the people’s plan to crown him gone forward, “it would have been no easy matter afterwards to wipe off the stain.” Meanwhile he “spent all that time in prayer” (Matthew 14:23; Mark 6:46), probably that the Father “might repress that folly of the people.” And the miraculous crossing, Calvin adds, would “profit his disciples by again confirming their faith,” and prove to the crowd next day that Jesus “had not been brought thither by a boat or ship, but… by his own power.”

The witnesses thus weigh the same evening two ways: as the hour of the disciples’ love, who could not bear to wait longer for their Master, and as the hour of Christ’s wise concealment, who let the crowd disperse and the tumult die. Both readings meet in the lesson that his apparent absence is purposeful — a withdrawal that tries the disciples’ faith even as it spares the people their folly, and that sets the stage for the deeper self-disclosure to come upon the water.
17:
The verse sets the scene of peril, and the grammatical-historical voices attend to its geography and its grammar. Robertson admires John’s “very graphic” tenses — the picturesque imperfect “were going” (ērchonto), then two past perfects: “darkness had already come” but “Jesus had not yet come to them.” Ellicott reads “the ship,” the one in which they first crossed, and “were going over,” the voyage “still continuing.” On the destination the commentators harmonise the Gospels: John says Capernaum, Matthew “the other side” generally, Mark Bethsaida (distinct, Ellicott notes, from the eastern Bethsaida Julias of Luke 9:10), and Clarke observes they in fact landed in the country of Gennesaret (Matthew 14:34) — driven off course by a storm he is willing to ascribe to “the prince of the power of the air,” all on the same western shore; Capernaum lay at the northern part, and “they went along the Galilean or western coast… expecting Christ to come to them.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, fills in what John omits from the Synoptics — the hours of struggle against the contrary wind, Christ appearing only “about the fourth watch of the night” (Matthew 14:25; Mark 6:48) — and corrects those who think the disciples had reached the far bank, since Bethsaida (near which Luke sets the miracle, Luke 9:10) and Capernaum “were situated on the same coast.” He cites Pliny and Josephus on the lake’s dimensions (some six miles broad and sixteen long) to show how far they could be driven without crossing it, concluding that “they did not go over so great a space by direct sailing, but through being driven about by the tempest.” From this he draws his pastoral point: “in this manner the Lord often makes his people fall into alarming dangers, that they may more plainly and familiarly recognize him in their deliverance.”

The witnesses also feel the pastoral weight of the scene. Here are the disciples, Henry observes at v.15, “in the way of their duty,” sent out by their Master and prayed for by him, and yet overtaken by darkness, distance, and a rising sea. The lesson the commentators draw is that obedience is no guarantee of calm: “the perils and afflictions of this present time may very well consist with our interest in Christ and his intercession.” The storm is not a sign of his absence but the setting in which his coming will be known.
18:
A short, descriptive verse, handled almost wholly by the grammatical-historical commentators, whose interest is the force of the tense and the framing of the storm. Gill pictures the plain sense: the sea “swelled, and was tumultuous and raging; the waves mounted up, and tossed the ship to and fro.” Ellicott and Robertson agree the imperfect should read “was rising” — the sea “then being agitated by the wind,” the scene “describing… as it took place” rather than a single completed event. Robertson parses the rare verb diegeireto, “a late compound to wake up thoroughly, to arouse,” with the genitive absolute “a great wind blowing” (anemou megalou pneontos). The verse thus belongs to the chain of vivid imperfects that, from v.17 onward, hold the danger continually before the eye — darkness deepening, Jesus still absent, the sea steadily climbing.

The one theological note is Barnes’s single cross-reference, which gives the storm its frame: it is the LORD who “raiseth the stormy wind, which lifteth up the waves” (Psalm 107:25). Read against that Psalm — to which Ellicott will return at v.21 with its “he maketh the storm a calm” — even the tempest that endangers the disciples lies within the providence under which the chapter’s whole sign unfolds. What the grammar shows as a rising sea, the cross-reference shows as a rising sea under God’s hand, so that the One who walks upon it in the next verses is no stranger to the wind, but its Maker drawing near to bring his people “unto their desired haven.”

The witnesses thus leave the verse poised between danger and deliverance. The rising sea is the climax of the disciples’ peril — darkness, distance, contrary wind, and now the climbing waves — and precisely the point at which, in the next verses, Christ will appear walking upon it. The grammarians’ insistence on the continuous imperfect serves this end: the danger is shown still mounting, not yet resolved, so that the reader, like the disciples, is held in the dark and the wind until the voice sounds across the water, “It is I; be not afraid.”
19:
As Christ appears walking on the water, the witnesses split between measurement and meaning. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the facts: the disciples had rowed “five and twenty or thirty furlongs,” about half the lake’s breadth — which Clarke and Ellicott, citing Josephus, give as some five or six miles wide and many more long — driven to their oars because the contrary wind forbade sails (Gill, Clarke: “as to sails… they could not now venture to carry them, because of the storm”). Robertson relishes John’s narration: the perfect participle elēlakotes (“having rowed,” the verb used of rowing in Mark 6:48), the accusative of extent placing them “in the midst of the sea” (Mark 6:47), the dramatic present “they behold” (theōrousin) “vividly preserving the emotions of the disciples,” the participle of process by which they watch Jesus “coming near the boat… slipping closer and closer to them on the water,” and the ingressive aorist that marks the moment “they became afraid.” The other Gospels explain the fear: they took him for a ghost (Matthew 14:26; Mark 6:49).

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the fear a parable of knowing Christ wrongly. It is, he allows, “impossible not to be seized with consternation and dread, when a spectre is presented before our eyes,” for we conclude “it is either some imposture of Satan, or some bad omen.” But John “holds out to us, as in a mirror, what kind of knowledge of Christ we may obtain without the word.” A bare “demonstration of his divinity” leaves us to “our imaginations,” so that “every person forms an idol for himself instead of Christ,” and the fruit is “trembling and a confused terror of heart.” Only when he begins to speak (v.20) do we gain “from his voice clear and solid knowledge, and then also joy and delightful peace.” The terror on the water thus prepares the comfort of his voice.

The witnesses note, too, that John gives the walking on the water its barest form. He records neither the disciples’ cry that it was a ghost, which Mark and Matthew supply, nor Peter’s venture upon the waves; his interest, as throughout the chapter, is less in the wonder itself than in the word that follows it. So the verse functions for John as the hinge between the storm and the saying — the sight that terrifies giving way, in v.20, to the voice that calms, and the bare miracle pointing past itself to the One who speaks the divine “I am.”
20:
The verse is brief — “It is I; be not afraid” — yet the witnesses find in it the whole logic of Christian assurance. The grammatical-historical commentators note the prohibition (mē phobeisthe) and the parallels in Mark 6:50 and Matthew 14:27, observing that John gives neither the ghost-fear nor Peter’s walking on the water that Matthew adds (Robertson, Ellicott). Barnes lets the cross-references carry the weight, hearing behind the words the divine self-identification that bids the believer not fear — “Fear not… when thou passest through the waters” (Isaiah 43:1–2), “Fear not; I am the first and the last” (Revelation 1:17–18; cf. Psalm 35:3). The very “It is I” (egō eimi) carries, for the attentive ear, the overtone of the divine Name.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the distinction that gives the saying its force. Confidence in Christ’s presence “belongs exclusively to the disciples of Christ”; the same words that calm believers “struck down” his enemies at his arrest (John 18:6), for to the reprobate and unbelieving “he is sent as a Judge… for their destruction,” and “they cannot bear his presence without being immediately overwhelmed.” But believers, “who know that he is given to them to make propitiation, as soon as they hear his name — which is a sure pledge to them both of the love of God and of their salvation — take courage as if they had been raised from death to life,” and “calmly look at the clear sky… victorious over every calamity,” taking him “for their shield against all dangers.” And he comforts them, Calvin ends, not by word only “but actually removes also the cause of the terror by allaying the tempest.”

The witnesses agree that the calm is wrought as much by the voice as by the stilling of the sea. Ellicott notes that Matthew’s fuller account here adds “the trial of St. Peter’s faith,” but John keeps the focus on the word alone: the same self-identification that will run through the discourse — “I am the bread of life” — sounds first across the dark water, so that the disciples learn in the storm what the crowd will be taught in the synagogue. To know who he is, the verse implies, is already to be delivered from fear.
21:
The verse’s close — they were willing to receive him, “and immediately the ship was at the land” — sets the chapter’s one real exegetical debate, conducted entirely among the grammatical-historical commentators: was the sudden landing a further miracle? Gill notes they “willingly received him… when they knew who he was,” the more welcome because “they were in distress.” Robertson reads the inchoative imperfect, “they began to be willing,” and holds it no contradiction of Mark 6:51 (“both Jesus and Peter climbed into the boat”). On the landing, Clarke leans toward wonder — the arrival seeming “extraordinary and miraculous” — while Barnes and Robertson resist: “immediately” (more exactly “straightway”) “does not of necessity imply that there was no interval,” only that it was short, as the same word shows in Matthew 13:5 and Mark 4:17.

Ellicott weighs the question most fully and decides against a separate miracle. Since v.19 places them “about half-way across the lake,” no appeal to their being secretly near shore “is consistent with the plain meaning of these definite words”; yet “the analogy of the miracles of our Lord does not lead us to expect the use of divine power to accomplish what was within the reach of human effort,” and the earlier Gospels would scarcely have omitted “among the greatest miracles” what John “mentions… only in a passing sentence.” He reads the words rather as a contrast — the labour and danger of the first half of the voyage against the “ease and rapidity” of the second, once Christ was aboard and the sea hushed, the “straightway” perhaps marking “the straight line of the boat’s after-course.” Behind it he hears the Psalmist — “He maketh the storm a calm… so he bringeth them unto their desired haven” (Psalm 107:23–30) — and Keble’s hymn, “Thou Framer of the light and dark.” For John, he adds, the whole sea-crossing, with the healings of Gennesaret that the Synoptics append (Matthew 14:34–36), leads back to the multitude and forward to the discourse at Capernaum.

The witnesses agree, in the end, that whether or not the landing was a separate wonder, the verse’s burden is the contrast between Christ absent and Christ aboard. While he was away, the disciples laboured against wind and wave and made little headway; the moment they received him, the voyage was as good as done. So the close of the sea-crossing preaches what the whole chapter teaches — that human effort, however strenuous, reaches its haven only when Christ is taken in; “many a doubting soul,” Henry adds, “is fetched to heaven by a pleasing surprise, or ever it is aware.”
22:
John’s convoluted sentence describes how the crowd, left on the eastern shore, came to infer that Jesus had crossed by no ordinary means; the witnesses divide between untangling the geography and drawing the lesson. The Eastern Chrysostom states the inference plainly: there had been only one boat, the disciples had taken it, and Jesus “went not with His disciples” — “what was this but to suspect that He had walked across the sea?” Augustine adds that “knowledge of the miracle was conveyed to them indirectly,” by the other boats that had come from Tiberias. The grammatical-historical commentators labour the same data: Calvin shows the crowd reasoning that, with the one ship gone without him and other boats arriving only next day, “it follows that he must have been conveyed across in a miraculous manner,” though he grants the words have “an intricacy and apparent confusion” (anakolouthon). Robertson lists “the three items these people noted” — the single boat, that Jesus entered not with the disciples, that they went away alone — and pictures the leaders “still lingering in excitement on the eastern side… next morning.” Clarke spends his note on the word peran, arguing it means not “beyond” but “by the side of” the sea (comparing Matthew 4:15), so that the crowd stood on the Bethsaida side; Wesley notes they “were forced to stay a while, because there were then no other vessels.”

Two voices read it for more than geography. Chrysostom marks the crowd’s shallowness — after so great a miracle “they did not ask Him how He had passed over,” unless their “when” really means “how.” Henry, in the Puritan vein, turns their search into a pattern for the soul: “those that would find Christ must diligently observe all his motions, and learn to understand the tokens of his presence and absence”; they “must seek till they find,” going “from sea to sea, to seek the word of God” — though he grants their zeal was still the “hot fit” of wanting to make him king (v.15).

The witnesses thus find in the crowd’s painstaking reconstruction a kind of unconscious testimony: by carefully noting the single boat, the disciples’ solitary departure, and Christ’s absence from it, the very people who sought him for bread bear witness that he had crossed the sea by no human means. Their reasoning establishes the miracle they will not pause to wonder at — proof, the commentators imply, that evidence alone never makes a believer, and that the same minds which can trace a wonder step by step may yet refuse the One it reveals.
23:
The verse is a parenthesis — other boats from Tiberias had come near “the place where they did eat bread” — and the grammatical-historical commentators treat it as such. Ellicott and Robertson explain that John inserts it (“howbeit,” alla) to account for how, where the evening before there had been only one boat, there were now several: boats had crossed from the western town in the morning, perhaps to a harbour or “centre of merchandise.” Gill, Barnes, and Clarke supply Tiberias’ history — built by Herod Antipas and named for the emperor Tiberius (mentioned in the New Testament only by John, 6:1, here, and 21:1). Gill identifies it with the ancient Rakkath, “the sea was its wall,” and notes it became a famed seat of Jewish learning where “the Misna and Jerusalem Talmud were written” and the sanhedrin sat after leaving Jerusalem. Barnes even adds a missionary’s nineteenth-century report of the ruined town (“now called Tabaria… occupied chiefly by Turks”) and its destruction “by an earthquake” on the first of January 1837.

Calvin, for the Reformed, fixes the sense geographically — the boats “sailed along that shore on which the multitude were standing… for the purpose of taking in passengers,” Bethsaida lying “half-way between Tiberias and Capernaum” — and dwells on the repeated note that “the Lord had given thanks.” The repetition “is not a superfluous repetition,” for it means Christ “obtained by prayer that those few loaves were sufficient for feeding so many people”; and “as we are cold and indolent in prayer, he presses upon us the same thing a second time.” Ellicott likewise observes that the act “had impressed itself upon the writer”: “because the Lord had blessed the bread it was that the multitude had whereof to eat.”

The witnesses thus turn even this aside to account. What looks a mere note on shipping carries, for Ellicott and Calvin alike, a reminder of the thanksgiving that made the loaves suffice — “the place where they did eat bread, after that the Lord had given thanks” — so that the prayer of v.11 is kept before the reader even as the crowd scrambles after more bread. The geography serves the theme: the boats from Tiberias gather the very people whose hunger for a fresh meal Christ will expose in v.26.
24:
The crowd, not finding Jesus, embarks for Capernaum to seek him, and the grammatical-historical commentators attend to the logistics and the motive. Lightfoot reconstructs their movement: they had come on foot to the desert of Bethsaida “by the bridge of Chammath, near Tiberias” (Mark 6:33), but now “sail back in ships… that they might follow Jesus with the greater speed,” and “perhaps that they might reach time enough at the synagogue: for that was the day in which they assembled.” Clarke and Ellicott note that not all five thousand could have found boats — “many others doubtless went thither on foot” — and that some of the Passover pilgrims would simply continue toward Jerusalem (v.2); if John’s “the multitude which still stood on the other side” is pressed, it names only the remnant. Robertson clarifies the long, involved sentence as a “resumption and clarification” of v.22: still not understanding how Jesus had crossed, “they acted on the basis of the plain fact” and “themselves got into” the boats.

Robertson then reads their hearts. They sought him, he says, with “a double motive apart from the curiosity” of the previous verses: they “had clearly not given up the impulse of the evening before to make Jesus king” (v.15), and they “had hopes of still another bountiful repast at the hands of Jesus,” as he will charge them in v.26. Barnes adds only that Capernaum “was the ordinary place of the residence of Jesus, and they therefore expected to find him there.” Their diligence in seeking, the witnesses agree, is real; but its springs are carnal, and the search that looks so earnest is bent on bread and a crown.

The witnesses read the whole bustle of boats and searching as an unwitting fulfilment of the day’s lesson. The crowd that would have made Christ king by force now scours the lake for him, mistaking the sign for a promise of perpetual provision; their energy is real, their understanding nil. Calvin’s judgment at v.26 hangs over the scene already — they “came like brute beasts to fill their belly” — so that the diligent crossing to Capernaum becomes the very approach Christ will meet not with another loaf but with the offer of himself, the bread that endures unto everlasting life.
25:
The crowd finds Jesus across the lake and asks, “Rabbi, when camest thou hither?” The grammatical-historical commentators locate the scene precisely: this is Capernaum, and the question opens the discourse delivered in its synagogue (so Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson, all pointing forward to v.59) — perhaps, Robertson suggests, “that very synagogue built by a centurion” (Luke 7:5). They note the courteous title “Rabbi” (compare John 1:38), now used where before they had said nothing. Robertson parses the perfect “when hast thou come?” (pote hōde gegonas), hearing behind it their baffled search: “we sought you anxiously on the other side of the lake and could not see how you came across.”

The interest of the verse lies in that puzzled question. Ellicott catches their amazement: “When and how could He have come?” — for “He had not gone in the boat with the disciples, and no other boats had crossed but those in which they themselves came.” Their “when,” several note, half conceals a “how,” the very wonder they will not pursue. Calvin adds the geographical correction that Capernaum lay “not far from where the river Jordan issues from the lake,” at the lake’s lower winding rather than directly across, so John’s “other side” is loosely meant — Tiberias on the broadest part, Bethsaida next, Capernaum near the lowest. The question thus marks the seam between the sign and its interpretation: they have crossed the sea to find him, yet, as the next verse will show, without understanding what they saw — and the searching “when” becomes the occasion for Christ to expose the “why.”

The witnesses also hear in the title a small irony. They call him “Rabbi,” teacher, and ask him a question of time and place, as though the wonder of the crossing were a riddle of travel; but the Teacher will answer the question they did not ask, turning their “when” into a “why.” So the verse, slight in itself, is the doorway of the discourse: the crowd arrives curious and well-fed, addressing Christ with courtesy but seeking him for bread, and his first word (v.26) will strip the courtesy to lay bare the craving beneath it.
26:
This is the turning-point of the discourse, and the witnesses are unanimous that Christ, refusing to answer their “when,” instead exposes their motive: “Ye seek me, not because ye saw signs, but because ye did eat of the loaves, and were filled.” The Eastern Chrysostom reflects on the manner of the rebuke. “The mild and gentle is not always useful”; a dull disciple must be roused “with a goad.” Yet Christ chides “very sparingly” — not “O ye gluttons and belly-slaves,” but gently, showing that he “desireth not honour from men” but seeks “one thing only, their salvation.”

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the diagnosis. Ellicott insists the word is “signs,” not “miracles”: the crowd had felt the wonders “as wonders” but never entered “the spiritual significance,” regarding the whole “from without” as “an eating yesterday, which may be repeated to-day.” Robertson calls it “a sharp and deserved rebuke” — they were “more concerned with hungry stomachs than with hungry souls.” Barnes turns it on the reader: to seek Christ only because “their wants were supplied” is “mere selfishness of a gross kind,” and many still seek religion “aiming at the loaves and fishes, and not at the honour of God.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, frames the fault most exactly. It cannot be denied that the miracles had drawn them; the sin is that “they sought in Christ something else than Christ himself.” The true profiting from God’s works would have been “to acknowledge Christ as the Messiah… to surrender themselves to be taught and governed by him,” and so to “aspire to the heavenly kingdom”; instead they “expect nothing greater from him than to live happily and at ease in this world.” Wesley, for the Wesleyan, reads the verse as a hinge in the ministry: “hitherto Christ had been gathering hearers; he now begins to try their sincerity” by the figurative discourse on his passion that follows. Cyril of Jerusalem alone looks past the rebuke to its remedy, cataloguing the scriptural ways to “gain this eternal life” — chiefly by faith and by the preaching of the gospel.
27:
The discourse’s first great imperative — “Labour not for the meat which perisheth, but for that meat which endureth unto everlasting life” — draws the witnesses to its grammar, its balance, and its closing seal. The grammatical-historical commentators correct the rendering: Ellicott urges “Work not” over “labour not,” preserving the verbal link with the crowd’s coming question “What shall we do, that we might work the works of God?” (vv.28–30); the sense is not “make no effort” but “let it not be the result of your constant working to have food which perishes.” Barnes and Clarke guard against misreading: the command forbids not honest labour (compare 2 Thessalonians 3:10) but “anxiety,” the making of bodily food “the main or supreme object” (cf. Matthew 6:25). The “meat that endureth,” Barnes says, is the gospel that “nourishes the soul to everlasting life,” citing Isaiah 40:31.

Clarke, in the Wesleyan key, presses the paradox of effort: though “our labour cannot purchase it… he that will have heaven must strive for it,” for working in salvation “is the way in which God chooses to give” it. And the note that “the Son of man… will give” this food (Robertson marks the future dōsei, the outcome still to be decided by their response) holds gift and striving together.

On the closing clause — “for him hath God the Father sealed” — the commentators converge. To seal is to ratify and attest (Barnes and Clarke liken it to sealing a letter or testament); God set his seal on the Son, Robertson notes, supremely “at his baptism when the Holy Spirit came upon him and the Father spoke” (cf. John 5:37; the rare divine sealing of Ephesians 1:13). Lightfoot supplies the richest background: the rabbis taught that “the seal of God is Truth,” which he applies to Christ, who is “the way, the truth, and the life” (John 14:6), the Father’s own truth “confirmed and ratified” by his seal.

The witnesses thus hold together what the crowd had torn apart. They had laboured across the lake for more loaves; Christ bids them work instead for the food that abides, which is himself, given by the Son of man whom the Father has sealed. Calvin adds that Christ “shows to what object our labour ought to be directed,” lifting their desire from the belly to the soul. The imperative is therefore not a denial of work but its right ordering: the same energy that chases perishing bread is to be spent in coming to the One who alone can give the bread of life.
28:
The crowd’s question — “What shall we do, that we might work the works of God?” — opens the discourse’s central exchange on faith and works, and the witnesses read the questioners’ hearts variously. The grammatical-historical commentators see in it the sinner’s native instinct. Barnes: “the idea of doing something to merit salvation is one of the last that the sinner ever surrenders” — they expected the way of life “by their works.” Robertson parses the deliberative subjunctive (“what are we to do as a habit?”) and allows “an element of vague sincerity… in spite of their supercilious attitude.” Ellicott notes they speak of “works,” an aggregate of deeds, where Christ will answer with “work,” a single principle. Lightfoot adds the rabbinic background of the labourer who “shall eat of those things wherein he laboureth,” bending it to the verse: the work that entitles us to the enduring food is to “believe in Christ, and ye shall feed on him.”

Henry, in the Puritan vein, takes it as “a humble serious question,” and praises Christ’s condescension in answering even interrupting questioners — teachers should be “swift to hear,” turning even “impertinent” questions to profit. Calvin, for the Reformed, is harsher: they “do not understand what they say, and talk without any definite object,” betraying “their ignorance of the grace of God,” for they do not see that God “bestows upon us, by the hand of the Son, all that is necessary for spiritual life.”

The Latin fathers, through the Catena, supply the verse’s theological depth — chiefly Augustine on the answer (anticipating v.29). To believe is not merely to “believe Him” but to “believe in Him”: “the devils believed Him, and did not believe in Him,” as “we believe Paul, but do not believe in Paul.” To believe in Christ is “believing to love, believing to honour Him… and be made members incorporate of His Body” — “the faith which God requires… is that which works by love.” Chrysostom, for the East, marks the obstacle: gluttony “makes the mind gross, and the soul carnal,” so that though Christ leads them “by ten thousand sayings,” they “continue groveling below.”
29:
Christ’s answer — “This is the work of God, that ye believe on him whom he hath sent” — is, the witnesses agree, the hinge of the discourse, and several call it the resolution of the whole faith-and-works question. The grammatical-historical Robertson quotes Westcott: “this simple formula contains the complete solution of the relation of faith and works,” and notes Christ turns their minds “from various deeds of the Pharisaic type” to “the central fact.” Ellicott presses the grammar — they said “works,” plural; he says “work,” singular — and concludes that “faith and work are one… as soul and body”: “the work of life is faith,” and “faith worketh by love” (Galatians 5:6). Barnes makes it plainly evangelical: this is “the work which sinners are to do; and doing this they will be saved,” for “Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth” (Romans 10:4; cf. 1 John 3:23).

Beneath this agreement runs a quiet debate over the phrase “work of God.” The Reformed Gill reads it as God’s own operation — believing is “of the operation of God, which he himself works in men… the pure gift of God.” But Calvin, also Reformed, expressly resists drawing that doctrine here: “those who infer from this passage that faith is the gift of God are mistaken; for Christ does not now show what God produces in us, but what he wishes and requires from us.” The sense for Calvin is exclusive — God “requires from us” faith alone, and “men toil to no purpose… if they have not faith in Christ as the rule of their life.”

Yet Calvin and the rest guard the balance: faith’s primacy “does not exclude either the love of our neighbour or any other good work.” Wesley, for the Wesleyan, sums the consensus — faith is “the work most pleasing to God, and the foundation of all others.” The single, God-required act of believing is thus named both the root of every work and itself the work that saves.

The witnesses agree, finally, that the verse rebukes both legalism and idleness at once. Against the crowd’s instinct to multiply “works,” Christ names a single “work”; but against any thought that faith is mere passivity, he calls believing itself a work — the soul’s active reliance on the One whom God has sent. So the saying became, for the whole tradition, the charter of the gospel’s answer to the question “what must I do?”: not a list of deeds to be performed, but a Person to be trusted, in whom doing and believing are made one.
30:
The crowd’s demand — “What sign showest thou then, that we may see, and believe thee?” — strikes every witness as astonishing on the heels of the loaves, and the grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the audacity. Ellicott frames the puzzle: “Do they demand a sign who had seen the thousands fed, and would then have made Him a king?” His answer is twofold — “a feeling soon quickened is soon cooled,” and Christ had now raised the claim to a spiritual power (the food that endures), of which they demand a fresh proof: “What is the work that He Himself does answering to the work of faith which He demands?” Clarke draws the sterner lesson: “even miracles themselves are lost on persons whose hearts are fixed on the perishing things of the world.” Barnes suggests the questioners may be other Jews — “rulers of the synagogue… who had not witnessed his miracles” — of the kind ever “asking for signs” (Matthew 12:38; 1 Corinthians 1:22).

The deeper note, as the verse turns toward the manna they will cite, is that their request is a bid to measure Christ against Moses. Having heard him claim to give the bread of life, they ask for a sign worthy of that claim — setting up the contrast Christ will answer in vv.31–32, where the true bread from heaven proves to be not Moses’ manna but the Father’s gift.

The witnesses underline the irony the demand exposes. Having just been fed by a miracle and having sought to crown its author, the crowd now asks for a sign “that we may see, and believe” — as if nothing had been shown. Clarke draws the lesson that signs are wasted on hearts “fixed on the perishing things of the world,” and Calvin that they ask in the spirit of those who will always find a reason not to believe. Their question is therefore less a request for evidence than a refusal of the evidence already given; the sign they lack is not in heaven but in themselves. And in demanding manna they betray that they want a Moses, not a Messiah — a giver of bread rather than the bread of God himself.
31:
The crowd presses its challenge with Scripture — “Our fathers did eat manna in the desert; as it is written, He gave them bread from heaven to eat” — and the witnesses agree it is a bid to measure Christ against Moses. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the background. Robertson and Lightfoot identify the controlling assumption: “the national expectation of the greater Moses,” the rabbis teaching from Psalm 72:16 that the Messiah would again bring manna from heaven. Clarke spells out the argument: “Thou hast fed five thousand… but Moses for forty years fed more than a million with bread from heaven: do something like this, and then we will believe.” Ellicott sharpens the contrast — their fathers ate manna “which came direct from God,” while Christ had given only “the food of earth… on the grassy sward” (the Psalm’s “bread from heaven,” Psalm 78:24; cf. Exodus 16:15).

Calvin, for the Reformed, exposes the carnality beneath the proof-text. Christ “put his finger on the sore” at v.26; now they “demand a Messiah by whom they are to be fed,” extolling the manna “cunningly, in order to bury the doctrine of Christ.” But when the Spirit names the manna “bread of heaven” (Psalm 78:24) it is not “as if God fed his people, like a herd of swine,” so they are “without excuse” in rejecting the soul’s true food. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, prepares the answer grammatically: the manna came from “the lower sublunary heaven,” to which Christ opposes the highest heaven, whence he says seven times in this discourse that he himself “came down.”

The witnesses agree that the manna citation is the crowd’s decisive misstep. They quote Scripture truly — “He gave them bread from heaven to eat” (Psalm 78:24) — but to a carnal end, demanding that Christ outdo Moses with a daily supply rather than receiving the One to whom the manna pointed. Wesley’s grammatical observation carries the theological weight: the manna fell from the lower heaven of the clouds, but Christ claims to have come down from the highest heaven, the eternal kingdom of God. So the very text the crowd wields against him becomes, in his answer, the type that he fulfils — the wilderness bread a shadow of the bread of life now standing before them.
32:
Christ’s reply — “Moses gave you not that bread from heaven; but my Father giveth you the true bread” — corrects the crowd on three fronts, and the witnesses divide the work neatly. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the grammar. Robertson hears “a blunt and pointed denial” that Moses was the giver, and marks two deliberate words: not “our” but “my Father” (the same claim that angered Jerusalem at John 5:17), and the present “giveth” (not the past “gave”), “a continual process,” yielding “the genuine bread” of which the manna was “merely a type” (on alēthinos see John 1:9). Clarke lays out the fourfold refutation — it was God, not Moses, who gave; the manna was a type, not the true bread; God now gives a better bread; and Christ himself is that bread. Barnes adds that Moses “fed the body… but he did not give that which would preserve the soul.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, fixes the precise axis of the contrast: it is “not to Moses and God, but to Moses and Christ.” The manna truly came down “the visible heaven,” the clouds, “but not from the eternal kingdom of God, from which life flows to us”; so the true bread is that “which quickens souls to a blessed immortality,” and Christ ascribes the gift to the Father “in order to procure for himself deeper reverence.” Lightfoot supplies the Jewish foil: the rabbis held the manna “was given for the merits of Moses,” against which Christ’s “Moses gave you not” quietly denies that it came “for any merits of his.” Wesley simply restates the verse, and Cyril of Jerusalem again points past it to the eternal life gained by faith and gospel.

The witnesses thus hear in the double “verily” the correction of three errors at once: that Moses, not God, was the giver; that the manna, not Christ, was the true bread; and that the gift lay in the past, not the present. Against all three Christ sets his “my Father giveth” — present tense, continual, and his own. Calvin’s point that the contrast is “Moses and Christ,” not “Moses and God,” guards the unity of the testaments: the same Father who fed Israel in the wilderness now gives the world the genuine bread, of which the manna was only the figure. The crowd asked Christ to rival Moses; he answers that he fulfils what Moses’ manna foreshadowed.
33:
“For the bread of God is he which cometh down from heaven, and giveth life unto the world.” The witnesses read this as the definition from which Christ argues, and dwell on its two marks — heavenly origin and life-giving power. The grammatical-historical Ellicott prefers “that which cometh down” (the open identification with himself waiting until v.35) and lays out the exact parallelism with v.32: “my Father giveth” answers “the bread of God,” “from heaven” answers “which cometh down,” and the present tenses show that “the bread of God ever cometh and ever giveth life,” and that life “is for the world,” not for one nation only. Robertson notes that this participle “coming down” recurs seven times in the discourse, and presses the decisive contrast (with Chrysostom): the manna gave “nourishment” (trophē) but not “life” (zōē) — “a most astounding statement to the crowd.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the logic and its sweep. Christ “reasons negatively from the definition”: since the manna had nothing of true, life-giving bread, the manna was not it. The verse “teaches that the whole world is dead to God, except so far as Christ quickens it, because life will be found nowhere else than in him.” Wesley adds the universal note — life “to the world, not (like the manna) to one people only” — and observes that Christ does not yet say “I am that bread,” else the Jews would not have answered him so respectfully in v.34.

The witnesses also feel the audacity of the claim. To say that the true bread “giveth life unto the world” is, Robertson notes after Chrysostom, “a most astounding statement to the crowd,” for the manna had given only nourishment, not life. Calvin presses the universality: the life is “for the world,” not for one nation, so that the gift outruns the very pride of descent on which the crowd had leaned in citing “our fathers.” And Wesley observes the discretion of Christ’s teaching — he does not yet say “I am that bread,” lest the offence come too soon, but lets the definition stand until, in v.35, he claims it openly for himself.
34:
“Lord, evermore give us this bread.” The witnesses are divided over the spirit of the request — sincere craving, carnal misunderstanding, or mockery — and the split is the verse’s chief interest. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as the carnal literalism of those who, like the Samaritan woman at the well, mistake the spiritual for the physical. Ellicott would render “Sir” rather than “Lord,” and hears them, “as the Samaritan woman,” thinking only “of the satisfaction of physical need” — they ate yesterday and “hunger again to-day,” and long for a perpetual supply (Robertson compares the woman’s “give me this water,” John 4:15). Clarke fills in the Jewish dream of a messianic banquet of manna, wine, and spiced oil that lay behind such a wish.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads them more darkly: “there is no doubt that they speak ironically, to accuse Christ of vain boasting,” charging him with their own unbelief. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, takes the gentler view — they mean it “still, in a literal sense: yet they seem now to be not far from believing.” Whether dull, mocking, or half-awakened, all agree they have not yet grasped that the bread Christ offers is himself — which the next verse will declare outright.

The witnesses read the request as the hinge between misunderstanding and revelation. Like the Samaritan woman’s “give me this water” (John 4:15), it is carnal in its grasp yet opens the door for Christ’s plainest word. Whether spoken in dull literalism (Ellicott, Robertson), in mockery (Calvin), or in the first stirrings of belief (Wesley), the petition asks for “this bread” without knowing that the bread is a Person. Christ will answer not by renewing the manna but by declaring himself, so that their half-formed longing for an endless supply is met, and exposed, by the great “I am” of the verse that follows. The same words, the witnesses note, may rise from very different hearts — and the discourse will sift them, gathering some to faith and scattering others to offence.

So the petition stands as the last word of misunderstanding before the great declaration. They ask for a bread they imagine will spare them toil and hunger forever, not yet seeing that the gift and the Giver are one. Christ’s reply will not correct their grammar but transform their request, answering the longing for “this bread” with the words “I am the bread of life” — and so turning a half-blind appetite into the occasion of the chapter’s central revelation.
35:
“I am the bread of life.” The witnesses receive this as the first of the great “I am” sayings and the open declaration toward which the whole discourse has moved — “this sublime sentence,” Robertson says, “was startling in the extreme to the crowd.” He notes the double sense: Christ is the bread of life because “it has life in itself, the living bread” (v.51), and because “it gives life to others,” and he lists the chain of Johannine “I am” sayings (8:12; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1).

The Eastern fathers read the saying first of Christ’s divinity.
