Chrysostom insists “He does not say this of His body, for He speaks of that at the end… here He is speaking of His Divinity,” the flesh becoming bread “by virtue of the Word.” Theophylact notes he says “bread of life,” not of mere nourishment, “for whereas all things brought death, Christ has quickened us by Himself,” and reads the doubled promise — never hunger, never thirst — as never being “wearied of hearing the word of God.” Augustine joins the two clauses into one: “He that cometh to Me… shall never hunger” means the same as “shall never thirst,” both signifying “that eternal society, where there is no want.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices dwell on the two verbs that answer the bread. Coming and believing are, with Augustine, the same act: “he that cometh to me” is “the first act of the soul in approaching Jesus” (Robertson), and “he that believeth on me” is its continuance (cf. John 4:14). Ellicott presses the analogy of eating: the loaves filled them only when “taken into hand and mouth,” and so “the spiritual bread… is taken by him who comes to Christ; it is eaten by him who believes on Him.” Clarke makes it personal — to come is to “receive my doctrine, and believe in me as the great atoning sacrifice” — and Gill, for the Reformed, that Christ alone “gives life to dead sinners” and sustains the life he gives. Calvin observes why Christ chose the metaphor at all: the analogy of bodily food lets us “perceive more clearly the grace of Christ,” penetrating the heart more deeply than a plain statement could, though even this figure does not fully express “the quickening power of Christ as we feel it.”
36:
“But I said unto you, That ye also have seen me, and believe not.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s solemn charge of inexcusable unbelief, and attend chiefly to two questions: what saying he refers to, and the force of “seen.” The grammatical-historical commentators note that no such words are recorded verbatim; Barnes and Wesley refer it back to v.26 (“in substance”), while Ellicott and Robertson allow it may point to an unrecorded saying or to the thoughts of John 5:37–44 — for “many words of Christ… have not been preserved” (cf. John 20:30–31). Ellicott corrects the emphasis: not “ye in addition to others” but “ye have even seen Me” — “the fullest evidence, amounting to actual seeing” — so that their unbelief answers their own demand for a sign in v.30 (compare Luke 16:29; John 20:29).

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the guilt: it is “extreme baseness” to reject “the doctrine of him whom they formerly acknowledged to be the Lord’s Messiah.” The “seeing” he takes not of bodily sight but of “their voluntary blindness,” for “they might have known what he was, if their malice had not prevented them” — Satan having “darkened their understandings” (cf. 1 Corinthians 2:8). The verse thus closes the section on a note of judgment, and prepares the turn to the Father’s sovereign drawing in the verses that follow — that, as Henry adds, though these believe not, Christ’s work “would not be in vain, for others would come to him and be saved.”

The witnesses thus read the verse as the section’s solemn close and its turning-point. The crowd has had the fullest evidence — they have “even seen” him, fed by his hand and taught by his word — and still they do not believe; their demand for a sign (v.30) is answered by the charge that sight has not bred faith in them. Yet, as Henry notes, Christ does not end in despair over their unbelief: he turns at once to the Father’s sovereign drawing and keeping of all that are given him, so that the failure of these hearers becomes the threshold of the gospel’s deeper comfort — that none who come to him will be cast out, because their coming is the Father’s work and not their own.
37:
The witnesses read this verse as the meeting-point of two truths the church has often torn apart: the Father's sovereign gift and the Son's unreserved welcome. None disputes that both stand here; the schools differ over the weight each bears.

The Reformed commentators press the first clause hardest. Calvin and Gill take "all that the Father giveth" as the whole body of the elect, handed to Christ in the eternal covenant as his portion and inheritance; their coming is therefore certain and none is lost, while the reprobate do not come — a truth Calvin says guards the authority of the doctrine when much of the world rejects it. Gill is careful that the "all" is neither the apostles as such (one was a devil) nor every individual, but the chosen given into Christ's hand.

The grammatical-historical expositors supply the syntax and a counter-emphasis. Barnes and Robertson note the collective neuter ("that which"), and Robertson hears in the strong double negative (ou mē) a definite promise to receive every comer. Yet Barnes insists the gift does not compel: God draws by Word and Spirit, opening the heart (Acts 16:14), granting repentance (Acts 11:18; 2 Timothy 2:25), so that believers are Christ's as the reward of his sufferings (Ephesians 1:4–5); still no one is saved against his will, but "made willing in the day of his power" (Psalm 110:3). He roots the coming itself in the Father's promise that Christ should see the travail of his soul (Isaiah 53:11), and the failure to come in John 5:40.

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices weight the second clause. Wesley reads the given as "all that feel themselves lost and follow the drawings of the Father"; Clarke turns "I will in no wise cast out" into a tender scene — the destitute man welcomed at the nobleman's door, the Gentiles received as freely as the Jews, no penitent ever refused. Ellicott names the reconciliation outright, citing Bernard and the Tenth Article: "if there is no free will, there is nothing to save; if there is no free grace, there is nothing wherewith to save."

The live debate, then, is over the "all": the fixed company of the elect (Calvin, Gill) or all who yield to the Father's drawing (Wesley, Clarke) — with Ellicott holding both in union rather than adjudicating.
38:
The witnesses take this verse as Christ's guarantee that the promise just made (v.37) cannot fail, because his whole errand is to do not his own will but the Father's. The agreement of the schools is near-complete; the interest lies in how each handles his "own will."

The Reformed readers (Calvin, Gill) make the verse a hinge of assurance. Calvin: the Son's only design is to fulfil the Father's commands, so he will never reject those the Father sends — "hence faith will never be useless." The apparent distinction between Christ's will and the Father's, he adds, is an accommodation to our distrustful minds, which invent diversity where there is none; Christ was manifested precisely to ratify what the Father decreed for our salvation, and so to "actually ratify" it in his coming. Gill guards the descent itself: "came down" is no local motion in one who fills heaven and earth, but the assumption of our nature, an act of amazing condescension.

The grammatical-historical expositors fix the grammar and the joy. Robertson notes the perfect katabebēka ("I have come down," a standing fact) and supplies the ellipsis — not that I keep doing my own will, but that I keep doing the Father's — calling this "the fulness of joy for Jesus" (John 4:34; John 5:30). Barnes reads it simply: he came for a specific purpose and will be faithful to the trust, so the Father's will is accomplished in salvation even if his hearers reject him. Ellicott holds the two truths together: having spoken of the Father's gift and of human action, Christ "once more identifies his own will with that of the Father, and yet states the fact of his possessing an independent will"; it cannot be that he should cast out any who comes, for he knows with divine knowledge "the infinite love of the Father," and "has himself come down from heaven to fulfil heaven's will in love to man."

The Wesleyan-Arminian note from Clarke broadens the welcome: to come "not to do mine own will" is to act not by human passion or prejudice — the very prejudice that would shut out publicans, sinners, and Gentiles — but by infinite wisdom and mercy that receives them. So a verse about the Son's obedience becomes, across the schools, a verse about the security of every soul that comes: the One who never seeks his own will cannot turn away those the Father gives.
39:
Here the Father's will is defined: that of all he has given, the Son should lose nothing but raise it up at the last day. The witnesses divide along the familiar line over whether "lose nothing" secures the elect infallibly or is conditioned on perseverance.

The Reformed voices read final perseverance. Calvin: Christ is not the guardian of our salvation for a few days but to the end, conducting us "from the commencement to the termination of our course" — hence the mention of the last resurrection, our one support while we lie, with Colossians 3:3 and Romans 8:36, as good as dead in the world, with "one foot in the grave." This promise, he says, is "highly necessary for us, who miserably groan under so great weakness of the flesh," that we may "boldly raise our eyes to the last day." Gill takes it as God's secret covenant will, the elect kept to glory. Barnes, here close to them, renders "lose nothing" as "I should not destroy": the believer dies and returns to corruption, yet Christ watches over him in the grave and keeps him to the resurrection of the just (Philippians 3:11).

The Wesleyan-Arminian readers insist the keeping is not unconditional. Clarke: God wills that every believer persevere and be raised, but "may will a thing to be, without willing that it shall be"; Judas was given to Christ (John 17:12) yet perished — God willed that he might be saved without willing that he must be, for he saves no man "as a stock or a stone," but "as a reasonable being and free agent." Wesley flatly glosses "all which he hath given me" with "if they endure to the end. But Judas did not" (John 17:6).

The grammatical-historical commentators frame the refrain. Robertson notes "the last day" sounds four times in this discourse (v.40, 44, 54) "with the majesty of a solemn refrain," and that here, unlike the general resurrection of John 5:28, only the raising of the righteous is in view. Lightfoot adds the Jewish background: they expected resurrection at Messiah's coming and denied it to the wilderness generation (v.31), so Jesus turns their hope toward himself as the bread that feeds to a resurrection life manna never gave.
40:
The witnesses see Christ here restating the Father's will in terms of human response — "every one that seeth the Son and believeth" — so that none may abuse election into fatalism. The schools agree the verse is the open, declared side of the secret will of v.39.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers stress that seeing is not enough; one must believe. Barnes drives the pastoral point: lest anyone say "if I am given to him all will be well" and sit unconcerned, Christ requires faith as the very evidence of being given — "had this explanation always been attended to, the doctrine of election would not have been abused." Clarke: it is God's will that every one who sees may believe and be saved; God gives the power, but "neither repents nor believes for any man." Ellicott marks the shift from the neuter "all" to "every one," singling out each unit in the mass, and the tense: the believer "hath" eternal life now (John 3:15; John 5:24), to be raised at the last day.

The Reformed voice keeps the secret and revealed wills together. Calvin: God ratifies his eternal decree by this very means — obedience to the gospel — so that those who bypass the appointed way and pry into predestination "desire to be saved contrary to the purpose of God." Election is hidden; God manifests it when he calls us to faith in Christ. Faith, Calvin adds, is itself "a sufficient attestation of the eternal predestination of God," and to demand more is to insult the Spirit who seals our adoption (Romans 8:15).

Wesley gathers the three verses into one: "this is the whole of what I have said... the eternal, unchangeable will of God," that every one who truly believes shall have everlasting life — the Jews saw and believed not, but "I will raise him up" effectually. Robertson notes the volitive future "and I will raise him up" as Christ's personal promise (v.54). Across the schools, the verse functions as Christ's own guard against both presumption and despair.
41:
The murmuring of "the Jews" draws the fullest patristic comment of the passage, gathered in the Catena alongside the moderns. The consensus is that the offence was not really intellectual but carnal: they had hoped for a bodily feast and recoiled when the food offered was heavenly.

The Eastern fathers expose the motive. Chrysostom: while they thought to get food for carnal eating they had no misgivings; the real cause of complaint was disappointment, masked as a scruple over his parentage — yet they still reverenced him for the miracle, so they only murmured. He notes they did not yet know of his miraculous birth, calling him "son of Joseph," and Christ does not rebuke this, since the marvel of his birth would have overwhelmed minds not ready even for his birth in the flesh (v.42).

The Latin fathers turn it inward. Augustine: "these Jews were far from the bread of heaven and knew not how to hunger after it" — the inner man's hunger was lacking; this bread requires the appetite of Matthew 5:6, hunger after the righteousness that comes down from heaven, not the self-righteousness they were busy establishing (Romans 10:3). He adds the mystery of the incarnation: he took flesh, but not after the manner of men, choosing a mother on earth while his Father is in heaven. Bede and the Gloss note that the same complaint recurs through the discourse.

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices locate the stumbling in his lowliness. Calvin: they were offended at "the meanness of Christ and his human condition," seeing nothing divine in one clothed with our flesh — yet this self-emptying (Philippians 2:7) was the very proof of his love; we sin in the same two ways, beholding him only with carnal eyes and then framing fresh imaginations to despise the gospel. Robertson catches the onomatopoeic egongyzon, a buzzing, cooing murmur, as these Galileans puzzle over his claim to be bread come down from heaven (vv.33, 35). Ellicott identifies the murmurers as the authorities, fastening on what they will not understand rather than on his explanation.
42:
The witnesses hear the murmur sharpen into a scoff: a known village family, a carpenter's son — how can he say he came down from heaven? The schools agree the objection is the perennial scandal of particularity, the human refusing to read the divine in the familiar.

The Reformed reading (Gill) marks the descent into mockery: from murmuring they pass to upbraiding his birth, intending to shame the claims he had made for himself as the bread of God and the bread of life — suggesting it "great arrogance" in him to ascribe a heavenly original to himself. The objection, on every account, is built from real but partial knowledge.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on that knowledge. Ellicott notes "Jesus Bar-Joseph" would be his common name; Joseph and Mary had been known to many in the crowd, the genealogies searchable, and for more than a year attention had been fixed on him — all pointing to "an ordinary life in a Galilean village. It is human, and therefore they think it cannot be divine. They can conceive a coming in the clouds of heaven — that would be a miracle and tell of God; but the birth of a child is no miracle! the existence of life itself, and such a life, is no sign! All this they cannot read." The mysteries of that birth were still stored in the mother's heart, awaiting the human life's completion before they should be revealed. Barnes simply gathers the Synoptic parallels (Matthew 13:55; Mark 6:3; Luke 4:22). Robertson catches the contempt in "this man": "they knew Jesus as the son of Joseph and Mary; they cannot comprehend his claim to be from heaven. This lofty claim puzzles sceptics today" (v.38).

The Catena and the moderns thus converge: the stumbling-block is not a flaw in the evidence but the offence of the Word made flesh (John 7:27). (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here treats Christ's tender humanity from another scene and does not bear on this verse.) The verse stands, across the schools, as the test-case of faith reading the heavenly through the earthly form — the same offence that will scatter even disciples before the chapter ends (v.66).
43:
A brief verse, and the witnesses keep their comment brief: Jesus, knowing their cavil, checks the murmuring and resumes. The little that is said still divides along the discourse's main seam.

The Reformed voice (Calvin) makes it a rebuke that throws the blame back on them: "My doctrine contains no ground of offence, but because you are reprobate it irritates your envenomed breasts; the reason you do not relish it is that you have a vitiated taste." The fault, that is, lies not in the word but in the palate that cannot receive it — a theme Calvin has pressed from v.36 onward. Gill notes Christ answered either overhearing them or, as God, knowing their secret murmurs and private cavils among themselves, and so "thus addressed them."

The grammatical-historical readers attend to the form and flow. Robertson: the prohibition mē gongyzete, present imperative, means "stop murmuring" — "there was a rising tide of protest" picking up the very verb of v.41. Ellicott observes that Jesus "does not meet their difficulty"; it does not even appear that it was expressed to him directly. Rather he "seeks to silence the interruption which their murmuring among themselves has caused, and resumes the discourse broken" off at v.40.

So the schools agree on the dramatic function — a pause that quiets the crowd before the weightiest words yet, the saying about the Father's drawing (v.44) — while Calvin alone presses the theological edge, reading the murmur not as an intellectual difficulty to be resolved but as the symptom of a heart not given by the Father, which no argument will satisfy. The very brevity of the verse, on this reading, is eloquent: Christ does not debate the scoffers but turns to unfold, for those with ears to hear, why some come and some cannot.

The witnesses thus leave the verse as a clearing of the ground rather than a concession: Christ does not retract a syllable of v.41, but stills the noise so that the decisive word may be heard. What the murmurers treat as an interruption, the Evangelist records as the calm of one who knows both the cavil and its source, and answers it not with defence but with deeper doctrine (v.44).
44:
This is the storm-centre of the chapter, and the witnesses know it: "No man can come to me, except the Father draw him." Every school affirms the necessity of grace; they part over the nature of the drawing.

The Reformed reading is the strongest. Calvin: faith is no common thing within man's will but a peculiar gift; "no man will ever of himself be able to come to Christ" until God approach him by his Spirit — a drawing "not violent, so as to compel by external force," yet "a powerful impulse of the Holy Spirit which makes men willing who formerly were unwilling." He rejects the gloss that "none are drawn but the willing," since the very willingness is God's gift. Gill agrees: men dead in sin have neither power nor will; efficacious grace must be exerted, for coming is believing and faith is the gift of God.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and much of the grammatical-historical school read the drawing as real but resistible — alluring, not dragging. Clarke, borrowing Augustine's trahit sua quemque voluptas, pictures a man drawn as a sheep by green herbage or a child by nuts: "drawing, not dragging" — none comes without it, yet none is forced. Barnes catalogues how God draws: enlightening the mind (v.45), inclining the will (Psalm 110:3), moving by motives, love, and the Spirit, "so that, while God inclines him, man yields without compulsion." Wesley: God draws "by the strong and sweet, yet still resistible, motions of his heavenly grace."

The Catena's Latin father supplies the deepest image and a both-and. Augustine: "if he who is drawn comes without his will, he does not believe" — but we are drawn by delight; "give me one who loves, who longs, who burns... and he will know what I mean." Robertson notes the verb helkyō, to drag a net (John 21:6) or sword (John 18:10), here moral power as in John 12:32 and Jeremiah 31:3: "the approach of the soul to God is initiated by God" — the other side of v.37 (Romans 8:7).
45:
Christ grounds the drawing in prophecy — "they shall be all taught of God" (Isaiah 54:13) — and the witnesses agree this defines the drawing of v.44 as an inward teaching, not an outward compulsion. They divide, predictably, over the scope of "all."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation and its reach. Barnes: "by the prophets" means the book of the Prophets, the reference being to Isaiah, though the same thought runs through Jeremiah 31:34 and Micah 4:2; the teaching shows the drawing "is not compulsory," the only obstacle a strong voluntary ignorance and unwillingness. Robertson notes the verbal adjective didaktoi theou, "taught of God," a free quotation from the Septuagint, and that hearing is not enough — one must heed and learn, "a voluntary response," yet "this one inevitably comes to Christ." Lightfoot sets it against the rabbis who read Isaiah's "thy children" as the disciples of the wise: rather, he who would come to the Son must be taught of the Father, "not those sorry fathers," learned in nothing but the traditions of men.

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices read it of inward illumination open to response. Clarke: "God draws men by his love"; he teaches a man to know his need and flee to the hope set before him — "fear repels, but love attracts," and he who preaches only terrors will never bring sinners to a God they dread (John 3:16). Wesley: "every man that hath heard" the secret voice of God, "he, and he only, believeth."

The Reformed reading presses the efficacy and narrows the "all." Calvin: the teaching is "not merely the external voice, but the secret operation of the Holy Spirit" — "the inward illumination of the heart" — and "all" must be limited to the elect, the true children of the Church. He draws the two clauses tight: as none can believe till drawn, so the grace that draws "is efficacious, so that they necessarily believe"; "these two clauses utterly overturn the whole power of free will." Thus the same text the Arminians read as universal teaching freely offered, Calvin reads as the effectual, particular instruction of the elect — the verse where the chapter's debate is sharpest, yet where all agree there is no coming to Christ but by a teaching that is God's own work.
46:
Christ guards the previous verse from misunderstanding: being "taught of God" does not mean seeing the Father, for none has seen him save the One who is from God. The witnesses unite in reading this as a claim to Christ's unique, essential knowledge of the Father — and so to his deity.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices press the Christology. Barnes: the addition guards against supposing one must literally see and hear God; that Jesus has seen the Father "implies that he is more than man" — the only-begotten in the Father's bosom (John 1:18), the brightness of his glory (Hebrews 1:3), a knowledge "which cannot be understood except by supposing that Jesus is equal with God." Robertson: with the eyes no one has seen God (John 1:18) save the Son who is "from God" in origin (John 1:1; John 7:29; John 16:27); "the only way for others to see God is to see Christ" (John 14:9). Calvin joins the two truths: no knowledge of Christ is had till the Father enlightens the blind, yet it is vain to seek God apart from Christ, for "all that knowledge of God which men think they have attained out of Christ will be a deadly abyss."

The Wesleyan-Arminian note (Clarke, Wesley) keeps it plain: God teaches not by appearing visibly but by his Spirit; "he who is of God" is Christ alone, who lay in the Father's bosom and saw and revealed him.

The Eastern outlier enriches the seeing. Cyril of Jerusalem qualifies it against the angels who "behold the face" (Matthew 18:10): angels see God not as he is but as far as they can bear; only the Son, with the Holy Spirit who searches the deep things of God (1 Corinthians 2:10), beholds the Father fully and reveals him "according as each can bear" (Matthew 11:27). So the schools converge: the inward teaching of v.45 reaches its end only in the Son who alone has seen, and alone can show, the Father.
47:
"He that believeth hath everlasting life." The witnesses take this solemn "Verily, verily" as the hinge that recapitulates the discourse and binds eternal life to faith — and, for the fathers, opens onto the bread that is Christ himself.

The Latin tradition (Augustine, with Alcuin and Theophylact in the Catena) reads believing as having Christ: "He that believeth in me hath me; and what is it to have me? It is to have eternal life," for the Word that was in the beginning with God is life eternal (John 1:4). Augustine sets the manna over against this bread — the fathers ate and died because they "believed what they saw, what they did not see they believed not" — and presses the distinction that will govern the eucharistic verses: "the sacrament is one thing, the virtue of the sacrament another"; to eat the heavenly bread spiritually is "to bring to the altar an innocent mind," while many receive from the altar and perish, "eating and drinking their own damnation" (1 Corinthians 11:29). Alcuin adds that such a man "dies not" but lives "for ever," not by faith only now but for ever.

The Reformed voice makes faith the organ of life. Calvin: "the reason we obtain life by faith is that we know all the parts of our life are contained in Christ"; he resists collapsing "believing" and "eating" into one — they differ "as former and latter," for "to him is an effect or work of faith," eating being the fruit of believing, not its equivalent. Against the Papists who "believe in Christ just as far as they think fit," he insists on a faith that grasps the whole Christ. Gill marks it simply "a certain truth, and to be depended on."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers stress the present possession. Ellicott: no man had seen the Father, but the Son stood before them in human form; "for the believer eternal life was not only of the future but of the actual present — he hath eternal life" (John 3:15; John 5:24). Clarke lays out the saved man's course — drawn, hearing, accepting, given to Christ, fed, persevering, raised, made partaker of life. Matthew Henry adds the contrast: the manna's advantage was small and temporal, but the living Bread is so excellent that he who feeds on it shall never die. Robertson, tersely: believing "is the way to see God in Christ."
48:
"I am that bread of life." The witnesses treat this short verse as a deliberate repetition of v.35, gathering the discourse to a head before the sharper claim of v.51.

The grammatical-historical readers note the resumption. Robertson: Jesus repeats "the astounding words of verse 35 after fuller explanation" — the believer has eternal life "because he gives himself to him." Barnes paraphrases: "my doctrines and the benefits of my mediation are that real support of spiritual life of which the manna in the wilderness was the faint emblem" (vv.32, 33). Ellicott draws the figure out at length: "the essence of life is unseen; bread is the visible form which contains and imparts it. The invisible God is the source of eternal life; the human nature of the Son of God is the visible form which contains and imparts this to the souls of men" — so the words that seem so hard to the murmurers (v.41) are only this truth cast in the form of their own demand (v.31).

The Wesleyan-Arminian note (Clarke) keeps to the means: "I alone afford, by my doctrine and Spirit, that nourishment by which the soul is saved unto life eternal."

The Reformed voice (Calvin) sees the repetition as purposeful: not satisfied with calling himself the life-giving bread, he "repeats the contrast between this and the ancient manna, together with a comparison of the men" who ate it, preparing the verses that follow. Across the schools, then, the verse is a pivot — the claim already made now stated bare, so that what comes next (manna that could not save, flesh given for the world) lands with full weight. The very plainness of "I am that bread of life" is, on every reading, the gathering-point of all that has gone before: the bread of God (v.33) is no abstraction but his own person, offered to be received.

The witnesses also note how naked the claim now stands: stripped of qualification, "I am that bread of life" leaves the hearer only two responses — to receive the person offered or to stumble at him. Gill simply refers the reader back to the fuller statement already made (v.35); the repetition, the commentators agree, is not redundancy but emphasis, the discourse pausing on its own centre before pressing on to the costlier word about his flesh (v.51).
49:
"Your fathers did eat manna in the wilderness, and are dead." The witnesses read the verse as Christ pressing home, on the Jews' own ground, that the manna they boasted in could not give the life he offers.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and grammatical-historical voices argue it from their own premises. Clarke: the Jews themselves held that the wilderness generation, which fell in the desert, would never have a resurrection (v.31); "ye acknowledge that your fathers who fell in the wilderness shall never have a resurrection — and yet they ate of the manna; therefore that manna is not the bread that preserves to everlasting life, according even to your own concession." Barnes draws the lessons: a real miracle was wrought for them, yet "the most distinguished temporal blessings will not save from death"; there is need of "that bread which cometh down from heaven and giveth life to the world" (Zechariah 1:5). Robertson, plainly: "the manna did not prevent death; this new manna will prevent spiritual death."

The Reformed reading adds two careful guards. Calvin: this says nothing against the manna "so far as it was a secret figure of Christ," which Paul calls spiritual food (1 Corinthians 10:3); Christ here accommodates his words to hearers who "cared only about feeding the belly" and "thought of nothing higher than this world" — for such, the manna was perishing food. And "not one of those who have once eaten Christ" loses the life he bestows, "as we stated under the Fifth Chapter" (John 5:24). Gill notes the pointed "your fathers," not "our," singling out those who ate it as common food, not as typical of the Messiah, and whom these their offspring so resembled.

So the schools agree on the argument and its edge: the manna fed the body for a generation and the eaters died; the true bread feeds the soul to a life death cannot hold (v.50). The boast that should have humbled them — "our fathers did eat manna" (v.31) — Christ turns into the proof that they need a greater bread than Moses gave.
50:
A short verse — "this is the bread which cometh down from heaven, that a man may eat thereof and not die" — and the witnesses comment briefly, all reading the promised "not die" as spiritual and eternal, not bodily.

The Wesleyan-Arminian voice is the clearest: Wesley glosses "not die" as "not spiritually; not eternally," distinguishing it at once from the bodily death the fathers suffered in v.49. The bread, that is, does not exempt the body from the grave but secures the soul against the second death.

The grammatical-historical reader marks the grammar and the wonder. Robertson notes the purpose clause ("that a man may eat thereof and not die") with its aorist subjunctives, and calls the whole "the wonder and the glory of it all, but quite beyond the insight of this motley crowd." Gill identifies the bread as Christ himself, that of which he had already spoken — the bread of God, the bread of life (v.33). Barnes simply cross-refers the promise of life to John 3:16, the great word that whoever believes should not perish.

The convergence is complete: the bread come down from heaven is Christ, and the eating of it — which the next verses will press to its costly meaning — secures a life over which death has no final claim. The crowd hears only an impossible riddle and will soon strive over it (v.52); the witnesses hear the discourse moving steadily toward the cross, where this bread will be given (v.51). The contrast with the manna is now complete: that bread fell and sustained for a day, and its eaters died; this bread comes down once for all, and its eater does not die. The brevity of the verse only sharpens the promise it carries — a life as enduring as the heavenly origin of the bread that gives it.

The witnesses leave the verse, then, as a quiet but absolute promise set against the manna's failure: that bread sustained the body and its eaters died, but whoever eats this bread "may not die." Gill, Barnes, Wesley, and Robertson agree the death denied is the death of the soul, and the life secured the life of the world to come — a promise the crowd cannot yet receive, but which the believing few will confess before the chapter closes (v.68).
51:
"The bread that I will give is my flesh, for the life of the world." The witnesses agree the discourse here takes its decisive turn — from bread to flesh, and from incarnation toward the cross — and here the schools' deepest division, over the Lord's Supper, first opens.

The Latin and Eastern fathers (Augustine, Bede, Theophylact in the Catena) read it both of the incarnate Word and of the sacrament: "the bread I will give is my flesh, which I will give for the life of the world" — given, Bede says, when he delivered the mystery of his body and offered himself on the cross. Theophylact presses a realist note ("not only the sign of Christ's flesh, but the very flesh"), yet Augustine at once spiritualizes: to receive this bread is to "be incorporated," to become the body of Christ and live by his Spirit — "O sacrament of mercy, O sign of unity, O bond of love."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices fix the reference on the atoning death. Barnes: "my flesh" is his body offered in sacrifice (1 Corinthians 11:24), for the world (1 John 2:2); the Hebrew idiom eased the move from bread to flesh. Clarke calls it plainly "a vicarious sacrifice and atonement for the sin of the world... one of the weightiest sayings in the book of God." Robertson ties "my flesh" to the incarnation (John 1:14) and "for the life of the world" to the Lamb who bears the world's sin (John 1:29; cf. Mark 10:45; Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:14). Ellicott, in fullest detail, hears the Passover setting (v.4): the emphatic "I will give" points to the future Crucifixion, the true Paschal Lamb whose flesh is the sacrifice for sin and the sustenance of life.

Calvin (Reformed) makes "flesh" the channel, not the source, of life: as the eternal Word is the fountain of life (John 1:4), his flesh "as a channel conveys to us that which dwells intrinsically in his Divinity," for in it redemption was accomplished — and he turns the verse against the Mass, where men usurp "what belonged exclusively to that one High Priest." Wesley keeps it spiritual and forward-looking: the discourse "refers directly to his passion, and but remotely, if at all, to the Lord's Supper."
52:
"The Jews therefore strove among themselves, saying, How can this man give us his flesh to eat?" The witnesses see the offence deepen from murmuring (v.41) to open quarrel, and they read the strife as a warning against the spirit, not merely the substance, of the objection.

The Latin and Eastern fathers (Augustine, Bede, Theophylact in the Catena) contrast the strivers with those who eat: "they who eat the bread strive not among themselves, for God makes them to dwell together in unity." Bede notes their crude literalism — they imagined he would divide his flesh into pieces and give it them to eat, and so, mistaking him, strove. Chrysostom turns the difficulty around: because they thought it impossible, Christ shows in the next verse that it is "not only possible, but necessary," pressing the demand harder rather than softening it.

The grammatical-historical readers catch the rising heat. Robertson: emachonto, an inchoative imperfect, means "to fight in armed combat," here a war of words — they were already murmuring (v.41), and now "began bitter strife with one another," "some probably seeing a spiritual meaning," the rest scornful; their "How can?" echoes Nicodemus (John 3:4, 9) but now in "scornful disbelief," as if charged with cannibalism. Ellicott: they have passed beyond the murmuring of v.41; they understand he means his flesh is to be eaten and to supply the principle of life, and "contend one with another as to how this can really be."

The Reformed voice draws the lesson. Calvin: the blame is not that they inquired into the manner — Abraham and the Virgin also asked "how" (Genesis 15:2; Luke 1:34) — but the "hardihood and eagerness to quarrel"; those who "dispute so keenly... block up against themselves the road to the knowledge of the truth." It is right, even important, to ask how we eat Christ's flesh, so far as Scripture answers; wrong to make the question a pretext for unbelief. Matthew Henry and Wesley note the degrees of testing — the Jews here, the disciples at v.60, the apostles at v.67 — "the flesh and blood of the Son of man" meaning Christ crucified and the redemption wrought by him, received by faith.
53:
"Except ye eat the flesh of the Son of man, and drink his blood, ye have no life in you." To the Jews' scornful "how," Christ answers not by retreat but by raising the demand — and here the eucharistic debate among the witnesses is at full stretch.

The Eastern fathers press the necessity. Chrysostom: since they called it impossible, he shows it "absolutely necessary," and answers the looming objection — that the eater still dies (John 8:52) — by the Resurrection. Cyril of Jerusalem reads the blood mystically of the Spirit and the new covenant cup. Origen sets it among the Word's many forms — to one water, to another wine, to another blood (John 7:38) — "as in his character as food he is living bread or flesh."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices insist the meaning is spiritual, not sacramental. Barnes argues at length that it cannot be literal: when the Supper was instituted the disciples did not eat his living body; the bread and wine were not changed "into the body, blood, and divinity," a notion he calls absurd; the plain sense is that by his bloody death he procures life for all who share in it, eating and drinking being figures of partaking (Matthew 8:11; Matthew 26:26-28). Clarke: this "can never be understood of the sacrament," not instituted till a year later, nor can the unworthy receivers who perished (1 Corinthians 11:30) be said to have eternal life. Robertson: "the only possible meaning is the spiritual appropriation of Jesus Christ by faith" (v.47). Calvin denounces as "a gross error" the ancients' use of the verse to demand infant communion — "this discourse relates not to the Lord's Supper but to the uninterrupted communication of the flesh of Christ which we obtain apart from the Supper."

Yet the Latin reading is not merely literal either. Augustine distinguishes the sacrament from its reality: the heretic and schismatic, cut off from the body's unity, may receive the same sacrament "but it does not profit them"; only he "who is in the unity of his body" truly eats the flesh and drinks the blood (Galatians 5:19-21). So the divide is real — Eastern/Latin sacramental realism versus Reformed and grammatical insistence on eating by faith — but Augustine himself already drives it inward.
54:
The negative of v.53 now turns positive: "Whoso eateth my flesh and drinketh my blood hath eternal life, and I will raise him up at the last day." The witnesses agree it advances from condition to possession, and from the "ye" addressed to the unlimited "whoso."

The grammatical-historical readers attend to the stronger verb. Ellicott: the word for "eateth" here (trōgōn) literally means to divide food with the teeth, "but this meaning is not to be pressed"; it is the present tense describing the ongoing process (vv.56, 58; John 13:18). Robertson notes the present participle "for continual or habitual eating," parallel to the continual believing of v.29, and warns sharply against the sacramental reading: to make participation in bread and wine the means of eternal life is "a violent misinterpretation of the Gospel and an utter misrepresentation of Christ" — bold imagery for "spiritual appropriation of himself who is to give his life-blood for the world" (v.51). Barnes again denies the literal sense and reads pardon and life procured by the atoning death.

The Reformed voice grounds assurance in the refrain. Calvin: the repetition confirms "that souls feed on Christ in precisely the same manner that the body is sustained by bread"; Christ joins eternal life with the last day "because our salvation will be hidden till that day." He concedes the verse is "figuratively represented and actually bestowed" in the Supper, which is "a seal and confirmation of this sermon" — yet denies the passage itself speaks of the Supper, since then all communicants would obtain life, whereas many "partake of it to their condemnation." Gill keeps it spiritual, "by faith, as explained in the preceding verse."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns the refrain into a triple disproof of sacramentalism: the Supper was not yet instituted; not receiving it cannot damn everlastingly; nor are all who receive it necessarily saved, since some at Corinth perished, "not discerning the Lord's body" (1 Corinthians 11:29-30). The schools thus converge on the promise — habitual feeding on Christ, and resurrection (v.40) — while parting over whether the Supper is in view at all.
55:
"For my flesh is meat indeed, and my blood is drink indeed." The witnesses read this as Christ confirming the reality of the spiritual food — true, not figurative in the sense of unreal — while the fathers and the moderns press that reality in different directions.

The Latin and Eastern fathers (Bede, Hilary, Theophylact, with Augustine and Chrysostom in the Catena) affirm a true partaking that yet must be understood spiritually. Bede: he shows "the great difference between bodily meat and the spiritual mystery of his body and blood." Hilary makes it the ground of mutual indwelling: "while we are in the flesh, Christ dwells in us by his flesh," and as we live by partaking of him after the flesh, so he lives by the Father after the Spirit. Theophylact guards against both errors: "we do not eat God simply... nor the flesh of man simply," but the flesh united to the Word, quickening "as heated iron remains iron" yet burns. Augustine again makes the eating the society of the saints, the unity signified by bread of many grains and wine of many grapes.

The grammatical-historical readers settle the text and the sense. Clarke and Robertson prefer the reading "true food / true drink" (alēthēs): as Christ is "the true vine" (John 15:1) and "the true bread" (v.32), so here he is the true meat and drink, by which those who receive the grace of his death "are really nourished... unto eternal life." Ellicott: this answers their "how can this man give us his flesh to eat?" (v.52) — he is the true elements of life.

The Reformed voice presses the cross. Calvin: as the body wastes without food, so the soul perishes if not fed with this heavenly bread; we are to "boast with Paul that we reckon nothing excellent but Christ crucified," for apart from his death we meet nothing but death — and so he turns again against "false expounders... who draw away souls from the flesh of Christ." Wesley keeps it experiential: "with which the soul of a believer is as truly fed as his body with meat and drink."
56:
"He that eateth my flesh and drinketh my blood dwelleth in me, and I in him." The witnesses take this as the heart of the matter: the eating is defined as a mutual abiding, the test by which true partaking is known from false.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the new note. Robertson: "abideth in me and I in him" is added in place of "hath eternal life" of v.54; the verb menō expresses "continual mystical fellowship between Christ and the believer" (John 15:4-7; 1 John 3:24; 1 John 4:16) — "there is, of course, no reference to the Lord's Supper, but simply to mystical fellowship with Christ." Ellicott calls it "one of those deeper thoughts which meet us only in the words of the beloved disciple": the union that results from the communication of life is not temporary but one that "remaineth" — "it is our home life, that of every day, and will be the life of the eternal home" (John 14:2). He sets it against the abiding wrath of John 3:36 and the not-abiding word of John 5:38.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke gives the vivid analogy: of all connections, none is so intimate and complete as that of food digested into the very substance of the eater; this our Lord makes "the model of that union which subsists between himself and genuine believers" — "he lives in them, and they in him," made partakers of the divine nature (2 Peter 1:4). Barnes lists the marks of indwelling: remaining in his doctrine and the benefits of his death, his Spirit and temper — his meekness, humility, and love — pervading the heart, supported by the consolations of the gospel (John 15:1-6; John 17:21-23).

The Reformed voice uses the verse to settle who really eats. Calvin: the only bond of union is that "our faith relies on his death"; this is no outward symbol "which many unbelievers receive equally with believers, and yet continue separated from Christ." Hence Judas, never a member of Christ, did not receive Christ's body though he took the bread — "faith alone is the mouth, so to speak, and the stomach of the soul." Augustine, gathered at v.60, says the same: the proof of having eaten and drunk is "if he abides and is abode in, if he dwells and is dwelt in, if he adheres so as not to be deserted."
57:
"As the living Father hath sent me, and I live by the Father, so he that eateth me, even he shall live by me." The witnesses read a chain of life: from the Father its fountain, through the Son, to the believer who feeds on him.

The Reformed voice traces the "three degrees of life." Calvin: "in the first rank is the living Father, who is the source, but remote and hidden; next follows the Son, who is exhibited to us as an open fountain, and by whom life flows to us; the third is the life which we draw from him." Christ makes himself the Author of life while owning that he received from the Father what he administers — yet "it is only with respect to his flesh that he compares himself to the Father" here; it is the Son "manifested in the flesh," not his bare Divinity, that is in view, and "it is not unusual with Christ to ascribe to the Father every thing Divine which he had in himself." Gill dwells on "the living Father," a title peculiar to the Father of Christ, who alone "ever did, and ever will, live as the Father of Christ."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the causal sense. Robertson: "I live because of the Father" — the Father "is the source of life" (John 5:26; Matthew 16:16) — and "because of me" for the believer, the same idea as "because I live ye shall live also" (John 14:19; John 11:25): "Jesus Christ is our ground of hope and guarantee of immortality. Life is in Christ." Ellicott prefers "by reason of": he that takes the Son into his own being receives the principle of life from him as the Son receives it from the Father.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the union to its height: "the union between Christ and his followers shall be similar to that which subsists between God and Christ" (1 Corinthians 15:22). So the schools agree on the structure — life is the Father's, the Son's by gift, ours by feeding — Calvin alone carefully restricting the comparison to Christ's incarnate office lest it touch his equality with the Father, the same guard Augustine raised at v.55: the Son does not grow better by partaking of the Father, as we do by partaking of the Son.
58:
"This is the bread which came down from heaven: not as your fathers did eat, and are dead." The witnesses read the verse as the deliberate close of the discourse, returning to the manna-contrast with which it began (v.31).

The grammatical-historical readers note the rounding-off. Robertson: this is the "summary and final explanation of the true manna (from verse 32 on) as being Jesus Christ himself." Ellicott: the tense is now past — "came down" — "pointing to his historic coming, because he has asserted that he is the bread" (v.33, 38); and "the discourse ends with that which has been the text of it." Barnes corrects the wording ("this is the bread, not that bread") and reads "shall live for ever" of the enjoyments of a better world, not of life on earth (vv.49–51).

The Reformed voice presses the polemic against Moses. Calvin: "there is no reason why you should prefer Moses to me because he fed your fathers in the wilderness, since I supply you with far more excellent food, for I bring heavenly life with me"; the bread is "from heaven" not by physical descent but because "it has nothing earthly or corruptible in its nature, but breathes the immortality of the kingdom of God." The manna had a twofold use, but these Jews, bent on feeding the belly, "beheld in it nothing else than bodily food," while "the life of the soul is not fading, but makes continual progress until the whole man is renewed."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley keeps it personal: "this is — that is, I am — the bread, which is not like the manna your fathers ate, who died notwithstanding." Across the schools the verse functions as a final seal: the true bread is the person of Christ, and to eat it is to have the life the manna could never give (v.51) — a close that gathers the whole sermon into a single sentence and sets it, once more, against the boast the crowd had begun with.

The witnesses note, finally, how deliberately the sermon closes where it opened: the boast "our fathers did eat manna in the wilderness" (v.31) is answered by "your fathers did eat, and are dead" — the same fathers, the same bread, now weighed and found wanting beside the bread that is Christ himself, who alone gives a life that outlasts the grave.
59:
"These things said he in the synagogue, as he taught in Capernaum." The witnesses treat the editorial note as fixing the scene and weighing the sermon — and, strikingly, as the prelude to mass desertion.

The grammatical-historical readers supply the setting. Ellicott, accepting the identification of Capernaum with Tell-Hûm, notes that modern excavation has likely uncovered the very synagogue — a Gentile's gift (Luke 7:5), of greater architectural beauty than was common among Galilean synagogues, with Corinthian capitals and a carved frieze among the ruins — and that "on one of the lintels of the door he may trace a sculptured pot of manna," connecting it with "the manna which the fathers did eat, and died." Robertson cites the same carved pot of manna and clusters of grapes among the remains, and notes this is the only use of "synagogue" in John save John 18:20. Clarke observes that the discourse from v.26 onward, though Christ was "repeatedly interrupted by the Jews," gave him "the fuller opportunity to proclaim the whole truth relative to his passion and death, to edify the disciples, and confute these gainsayers."

The Reformed voice draws the sober lesson. Calvin: John names the place so that we may know "there were many present," and that a sermon was delivered "as on a weighty and important subject" — yet "out of so great a multitude there were scarcely to be found a very few who profited by it," and it proved "the occasion of desertion to many who professed to be disciples of Christ." When they rise up in crowds and conspire against him, he warns, "let this narrative be deeply impressed on our minds, that we may never murmur against Christ when he speaks; and if in the present day we perceive any thing of this kind in others, let not their pride disturb our faith."

So a plain note of place becomes, for the witnesses, a hinge: the fullest sermon, preached in the most public place and at the very synagogue whose lintel bore the manna, sifting the crowd that v.60 will scatter — the place of the boast "our fathers did eat manna" made the place of its undoing.
60:
"This is a hard saying; who can hear it?" Now the offence spreads to the disciples themselves, and the witnesses give the verse rich comment, agreeing the hardness lay in carnal hearing, not in any fault of the word.

The Latin and Eastern fathers (Augustine, Chrysostom, Theophylact, Alcuin, Bede in the Catena) probe the murmur. Augustine: "if his disciples thought that saying hard, what would his enemies think?" — yet "God's mysteries should draw men's attention, not enmity." Chrysostom: "hard" means "difficult to receive, too much for their weakness"; they thought he spoke "more loftily than he had a right to." Theophylact warns these were not true disciples but hangers-on, who only seemed to be receiving instruction. The fathers gather the whole of vv.62–65 here: the ascension (v.62) would prove his descent and remove the gross literal eating; "it is the spirit that quickeneth, the flesh profiteth nothing" (v.63) means his words must be taken spiritually; and unbelief, not difficulty, was the true cause — "no man can come unto me except it were given him of my Father" (v.65).

The Puritan Henry expounds the murmur at length: "if it is to be understood figuratively, it is not intelligible; if literally, not practicable. What! must we turn cannibals?" Had they "humbly begged of Christ to declare unto them this parable, he would have opened it, and their understandings too; for the meek will he teach his way." But they were unwilling to have it explained, "because they would not lose this pretence for rejecting them — that they were hard sayings."

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices read the sifting. Calvin: it is monstrous that those professing discipleship desert in crowds; "if we perceive anything of this kind in others, let not their pride disturb our faith." Robertson and Barnes note these "went back" (v.66) from prior allegiance. But the deepest reading is again Augustine's: "to be carnally-minded is death"; the Lord gives his flesh to eat, "yet to understand it according to the flesh is death" — "therefore we ought not to understand the flesh carnally." The verse stands as the turning-point where the discourse divides the crowd.
61:
"When Jesus knew in himself that his disciples murmured at it, he said unto them, Doth this offend you?" The witnesses dwell on the knowledge — Christ's reading of secret hearts — and on the gentleness of the question.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the fullest treatment. Their cavils were "secret in their own breasts, or whispered among themselves in a corner," but Christ "knew them; he saw them, he heard them." He took notice "not only of the bold and open defiances... but of the secret slights that are put upon his doctrine by carnal professors." And he knew it "in himself, not by any information given him, nor any external indication... but by his own divine omniscience" — not as the prophets by revelation (for that was sometimes hid from them, 2 Kings 4:27) "but by a divine knowledge in him": he is "that essential Word that discerns the thoughts of the heart" (Hebrews 4:12-13); "thoughts are words to Christ." The question "Doth this offend you?" Henry reads with wonder: "see how people by their own wilful mistakes create offences to themselves; they take offence where there is none given, and even make it where there is nothing to make it of."

The grammatical-historical reader keeps it briefer. Ellicott (with Robertson) marks that the murmuring was "among themselves" (v.60) and that Jesus, knowing it within himself, meets it directly — Alcuin in the Catena glossing "this" as the command to eat his flesh and drink his blood.

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the omniscience as proof of his deity. Gill notes he knew "their secret murmurs and private cavils among themselves"; Calvin sees in it the searching insight that distinguishes the believing from the unbelieving heart. So the schools converge on a small but weighty point — the searching knowledge of Christ, which makes the offence the hearers' own creation, not the word's fault, and which v.64 will turn into the open verdict that some of them believe not.

The witnesses thus make the verse a quiet display of deity in the act of pastoral care: the same Christ who reads the unspoken murmur stoops to ask rather than to condemn, giving the offended a chance to be undeceived. The wonder of "Doth this offend you?" is, for Henry, that the offence was needless — taken "where there is none given" — and the gentleness of the question is itself an appeal to reconsider before they go back (v.66).
62:
"What and if ye shall see the Son of man ascend up where he was before?" The witnesses read the unfinished question two ways — as a yet harder demand on faith, and as the very proof that would resolve the offence.

The Eastern father leads. Chrysostom: the revelation of his ascent "was a mark of his Divinity"; had he merely said he came down from heaven without adding more, "he would have offended his hearers more," but by saying that his flesh is the life of the world, that he was sent by the living Father and lives by the Father, and at last that he came down, "he removed all doubt." So long as they thought him Joseph's son they could receive nothing, but if once they believed he came down from heaven and would ascend thither, "they would be much more willing and able to admit" his doctrines. Cyril of Jerusalem (cited at v.69) sets the ascension within the larger hope of his coming.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the logic. Ellicott and Robertson take the ascension as the crowning sign; Barnes and Clarke note that to see him ascend would prove that he came down. Calvin: the sight of his ascent should remove the offence, for it shows his origin is heavenly and his whole errand divine.

The Puritan Henry lays out the senses fairly: "if I should tell you of that, surely it would much more offend you" — those who stumble at smaller difficulties should consider how they will get over greater (John 3:12); or, with Whitby, when they see him ascend "my body will be less capable of being eaten by you in that gross sense wherein you now understand it"; or, when they see it or hear it from eye-witnesses, "surely then you will be satisfied," for "if he ascended, certainly he descended" (Ephesians 4:9-10). The Catena's Augustine adds the Christological point: "the Son of man in heaven, as he was Son of God upon earth" — one person, that we believe "not a quaternity, but a Trinity." Wesley keeps it short: if this offend you, how will you bear what is harder?
63:
"It is the spirit that quickeneth; the flesh profiteth nothing: the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life." The witnesses treat this as Christ's own key to the whole discourse — and divide over whose "flesh" profits nothing.

The Eastern and Latin fathers guard his flesh. Chrysostom: "you ought to understand my words spiritually; he who understands them carnally is profited nothing" — and "in saying the flesh profits nothing, he does not speak of his own flesh... but that of the carnal hearer." Augustine agrees: they had understood his flesh "as a carcase to be cut up"; "join the spirit to the flesh, and it profits much, for if the flesh profited not, the Word would not have become flesh" (John 1:14); the flesh cleanses "not of itself, but by the Word who assumed it." "If you understand them spiritually, they are spirit and life to you; if carnally, even then they are spirit and life, but not to you."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices read the contrast of principles. Robertson and Barnes take "the flesh" as the carnal, literal mode of understanding that profits nothing, over against the Spirit who gives life. Calvin: the words are "spiritual, having nothing carnal in them." Gill and Clarke turn it on the carnal mind that cannot relish spiritual things.

The Puritan Henry draws the threefold lesson: the bare ordinance, unless the Spirit quicken, "profits nothing... as food to a dead man"; the doctrine of eating Christ's flesh, taken literally, "profits nothing, but rather leads us into mistakes," while its spiritual meaning "quickens the soul" — to believe that Christ died for me, and draw strength from it, "this is the spirit and life of that saying"; and "those that are in the flesh profit not by Christ's discourses" (1 Corinthians 2:14-15). Wesley, briefly: "the words... are spirit" — to be taken in their spiritual sense (v.68). The schools thus agree the key is spiritual hearing; they differ only over whether "flesh" names the carnal hearer (the fathers) or the carnal mode of reading (the moderns).
64:
"But there are some of you that believe not." The witnesses read Christ's foreknowledge of the unbelieving — and of his betrayer — as the explanation of the murmuring, and a fresh proof of his deity.

The Latin father (Augustine, gathered at v.60) gives the reason of the unbelief: "he says not, there are some among you who understand not, but gives the reason why they do not understand" — "except you believe, you shall not understand"; the adversary, "though he avert not his face, yet closes his mind to the ray of light which should penetrate him." And the Evangelist's note that "Jesus knew from the beginning" proves that "he knew all things before the foundation of the world," a proof of his divinity.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the foreknowledge. Robertson and Barnes note Christ knew from the first who believed not and who should betray him — an "insight" that "shows his high nature." Ellicott marks that he excludes the genuine disciples from the charge — "some," not all.

The Puritan Henry expounds it as the fulfilment of Isaiah's "who hath believed our report?" (Isaiah 53:1): "among those who are nominal Christians, there are many who are real infidels," and "the unbelief of hypocrites, before it discovers itself to the world, is naked and open before the eyes of Christ" — he knew "who of the multitudes that followed him believed, and who of the twelve should betray him," even "when they were in the hottest pang of their zeal." He draws the careful guard: the apostasy of long professors "is a certain proof of their constant hypocrisy... but is not a proof of the possibility of the total and final apostasy of any true believers" (1 John 2:19) — "the star that falls never was a star." The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices agree on the omniscience while keeping their accent: Calvin sees the secret discrimination of elect from reprobate; Wesley and Clarke, that the unbelief is the hearers' own, foreknown but not forced. The verse seals the discourse's diagnosis: not the difficulty of the truth but the unbelief of the heart drove them away (v.65).
65:
"Therefore said I unto you, that no man can come unto me, except it were given unto him of my Father." The witnesses hear Christ repeat the lesson of v.44, now as the explanation of unbelief, and the chapter's debate over grace returns a last time.

The Latin father makes it the doctrine of grace. Augustine (at v.60): "so then our faith is given to us, and no small gift it is. Wherefore rejoice if you believe; but be not lifted up, for what have you which you did not receive?" That this grace is given to some and not to others "no one can doubt, without going against the plainest declarations of Scripture"; and why it is not given to all "cannot disquiet the believer," who knows that all are justly liable to condemnation, and that it is of God's great mercy only that any are pardoned — why one rather than another "rests with him, whose judgments are unsearchable" (Romans 11:33).

The Reformed voice presses the same. Calvin: coming is the Father's gift, so Christ "could not but know who believed and who did not, because faith is the gift and work of God, and all his Father's gifts and works could not but be known to him." Gill reads the moral impotence of fallen man, unable to come without given grace and a heart to come.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and Puritan readers hold the gift together with response. Clarke: the unbelief is the hearers' own, yet none comes without the Father's gift of grace. Henry ties it back to v.44: "there he had said that none could come to him except the Father draw him; here he saith, except it be given him of my Father, which shows that God draws souls by giving them grace and strength, and a heart to come, without which, such is the moral impotency of man in his fallen state, that he cannot come." So the verse restates the necessity of grace on which all the schools agree, while the old division — whether the gift is efficacious for the elect, or grace offered to be received — sounds once more beneath their consent.
66:
"From that time many of his disciples went back, and walked no more with him." The witnesses read the great defection as the discourse's bitter fruit — and a perennial warning.

The Latin and Eastern fathers (Augustine, Chrysostom in the Catena) mark the nature of the fall. Chrysostom: "he does not say withdrew, but went back — i.e. from being good hearers, from the belief which they once had." Augustine: "being cut off from the body, their life was gone... they were created among the unbelieving"; they went back "after Satan, not after Christ." Yet, the fathers add, it was "very useful and necessary" that he spoke words which did not edify these, for it exposed hearts that fed only on the belly and reminded them that all those miracles were typical.

The Puritan Henry expounds the backsliding fully: "many of them went back to their houses, and families, and callings... one to his farm and another to his merchandise" — like Orpah turning back "to her people, and to her gods" (Ruth 1:15). "When some backslide many backslide with them; the disease is infectious." He marks the occasion — "from the time that Christ preached this comfortable doctrine, that he is the bread of life" — and the lesson: "the corrupt and wicked heart of man often makes that an occasion of offence which is indeed matter of the greatest comfort"; "men's humours must be captivated to God's word, and not God's word accommodated to men's humours." The degree is total: "they walked no more with him," and it is hard for such, once enlightened, to be renewed again to repentance (Hebrews 6:4-6).

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices keep the sober note. Calvin: "though the faith of some be overthrown, yet the foundation of God stands sure"; Robertson and Barnes read the apostasy of nominal disciples whose belief was never real. Across the schools the verse stands as the dark counterpart to the apostles' confession that follows (v.68): the same word that scattered the many drew the few closer.

The witnesses leave the verse as the chapter's darkest turn, yet not its last word: the defection is real and grievous, but it sifts rather than overthrows the church, separating the disciples "at large" from the twelve who remain. Henry's caution stands over it — "the apostasy of Christ's disciples from him, though really a strange thing, yet has been such a common thing that we need not be surprised at it" — and the chapter at once turns to the few who do not go (v.67).
67:
"Then said Jesus unto the twelve, Will ye also go away?" The witnesses read the question as both a test and a means of fixing the faithful few, spoken with affecting tenderness.

The Eastern father reads the pastoral wisdom. Chrysostom (at v.60): "this was the right way to retain them." Had he praised them, "they would naturally, as men do, have thought that they were conferring a favour upon Christ by not leaving him"; by showing that "he did not need their company, he made them hold the more closely by him." He does not say "go away," which "would have been to cast them off," but asks whether they wish to, "thus preventing their staying with him from any feeling of shame or necessity: for to stay from necessity would be the same as going away."

The Puritan Henry expounds the double edge. "He saith nothing to those who went back. If the unbelieving depart, let them depart"; but he takes this occasion "to speak to the twelve, to confirm them, and by trying their stedfastness the more to fix them." On the one hand, "it is at your choice whether you will or no" — "Christ will detain none with him against their wills; his soldiers are volunteers, not pressed men" (Joshua 24:15). On the other, "it is at your peril if you do go away," for "the nearer we have been to Christ and the longer we have been with him... the greater will be our sin if we desert him" — "Should such a man as I flee?" (Nehemiah 6:11).

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices keep it brief: Calvin and Gill note the question proves and confirms their steadfastness; Barnes and Robertson, that it casts none off but searches all. So the verse turns the chapter from defection to confession — the same sifting word that scattered the many now drawing from the twelve the answer of v.68, and exposing, even among them, the one who would betray (v.70).

The witnesses see in the question, then, neither anxiety nor indifference but searching love: Christ would have no disciple held by shame, habit, or crowd, and so offers the twelve their liberty precisely at the hour of temptation. By refusing to compel, he secures a steadfastness that is freely given — the very freedom that makes Peter's answer, when it comes, a confession and not a mere convenience (v.68).
68:
"Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the words of eternal life." Peter's answer for the twelve draws warm comment, the witnesses reading it as the model of a faith that clings to Christ for want of any other refuge — and for love of him.

The Eastern father (Chrysostom, at v.60) calls it "a speech of the greatest love, proving that Christ was more precious to them than father or mother." Lest it seem they stayed only because there was no one whose guidance they could look to, Peter adds "thou hast the words of eternal life" — which "showed that he remembered his Master's words, I will raise him up, and, hath eternal life" (v.40, 54). The Latin Augustine glosses the cry: "as if he said, You cast us from you? give us another to whom we shall go, if we leave you."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices weigh the confession's substance. Barnes and Robertson note Peter contrasts the words of eternal life (v.63) with every alternative — there is simply no one else; Calvin and Gill read the genuine faith of the elect over against the apostasy just recorded (v.66). Ellicott marks the deliberate echo of the discourse — the "words" that Christ has just said are spirit and life.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley keeps it plain. Across the schools the verse is the hinge of recovery: where the many heard a hard saying and left (v.60), Peter hears the words of eternal life and stays, the very saying that offended the crowd becoming the confession of the church. The faith that owns there is no one else to go to is, the witnesses agree, the faith that has truly heard and been taught of the Father (v.45), and that will go on to confess him in v.69.

The witnesses thus hear in Peter's question the logic of all true perseverance: not that the saying had grown easy, but that there was no other to whom one could go. Every alternative — the law of Moses, the traditions of the fathers, the bread that perishes — had already been weighed in this very discourse and found unable to give life. The disciple stays, Augustine and Chrysostom agree, because he has nowhere better to go and Someone better than all to hold (v.69).
69:
"And we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ, the Son of the living God." The witnesses read Peter's confession as the positive answer of faith — believing issuing in knowing — and the foil to the Jews' "is not this the son of Joseph?" (v.42).

The Latin father states the order of faith and knowledge. Augustine (at v.60): "for we believed, in order to know. Had we wished first to know, and then to believe, we could never have been able to believe." The confession is "that thou art eternal life, and that in thy flesh and blood thou givest what thou art thyself" — binding Peter's words back to the bread of the discourse (v.51).

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the two verbs. Barnes and Robertson note the perfect tense — "we have believed and have come to know" — faith ripening into assured knowledge; Ellicott marks the contrast with the murmurers who knew only his earthly origin. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem sets the confession of "the Son of the living God" within the church's rule of faith, the Son who, with the Holy Spirit, alone knows and reveals the Father (Matthew 16:16; Matthew 11:27).

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the substance of the creed. Calvin and Gill take it as the confession of true and saving faith in the elect, who do not waver when the crowd deserts; Wesley and Clarke, the open avowal that answers Christ's searching question (v.67). So the verse completes the recovery begun in v.68: the twelve, sifted from the crowd, name him not Joseph's son but the Christ, the Son of the living God — the discourse on the living bread answered by a confession of the living Son (v.57), even as the next verse will remind them that the confession of the lips is no guarantee of the heart (v.70).

The witnesses note how the confession gathers the whole discourse into a creed: he who is the bread come down from heaven (v.33), whose flesh is given for the world (v.51), is owned as "the Christ, the Son of the living God." Where the crowd had stalled at "the son of Joseph" (v.42), the twelve, taught of the Father (v.45), confess the living Son of the living Father (v.57).
70:
"Have not I chosen you twelve, and one of you is a devil?" To Peter's "we believe," Christ adds a sobering exception, and the witnesses read it as a check on presumption and a window onto election and evil.

The Eastern father reads the pastoral aim. Chrysostom (at v.60): "do not suppose that, because you have followed me, I shall not reprove the wicked among you." Yet he does not say "one of you shall betray me," but "is a devil," so that "they did not know what the speech meant, and thought that it was only a case of wickedness in general" — Christ "neither, by exposing him, makes him shameless and contentious; nor again emboldens him, by allowing him to think himself concealed."

The Latin father turns it to providence and free will. Augustine: Judas "was elected to be an involuntary and unconscious instrument of producing the greatest good" — "as the wicked turn the good works of God to an evil use, so reversely God turns the evil works of man to good"; Christ "made a good use of his wickedness, allowing himself to be betrayed that he might redeem us." And on election: "his election does not impose any necessity upon the person with respect to the future, but leaves it in the power of his will to be saved or perish." Bede distinguishes the purposes: "he elected the eleven for one purpose, the twelfth for another — the eleven to fill the place of apostles, and persevere to the end; the twelfth to the service of betraying him, which was the means of saving the human race."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed, and Wesleyan-Arminian voices keep their accents. Robertson and Barnes note the shock of a chosen one called "a devil"; Calvin and Gill read it consistent with sovereign choice to office without choice to salvation; Wesley insists election to apostleship imposed no necessity of sin. The debate is sharpest here: how a man "chosen" by Christ becomes a devil — Augustine and Bede answering by distinguishing the ends of election, Wesley by guarding human freedom, all agreeing the betrayal served the world's redemption (v.71).
71:
The Evangelist names the betrayer: "He spake of Judas Iscariot, the son of Simon, for he it was that should betray him, being one of the twelve." The witnesses read John's note as closing the chapter on a deliberate, foreknown treachery in the inmost circle.

The grammatical-historical readers attend to the identification and its weight. Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, and Clarke mark the pointed "one of the twelve" — the betrayal came not from the deserting crowd (v.66) but from the chosen band, heightening both the foreknowledge of Christ (v.64) and the horror of the act. Robertson notes John's settled habit of identifying Judas in just this way whenever he names him.

The Reformed voice keeps it within the providence already stated. Calvin and Gill read the verse as confirming that Christ knew from the first whom he had chosen and to what end, the betrayal serving, not thwarting, the purpose of redemption (v.70). The chapter that opened with a multitude fed on the hillside closes with the naming of the one who would sell the Bread of life — "one of the twelve."

So the discourse on the living bread ends in shadow and light together: the many gone back (v.66), the eleven confessing (v.69), and the one marked out whose treachery would, in the strange economy the fathers traced, become the means of the very life of the world (v.51). The witnesses leave the verse as John wrote it — a sober, unflinching record that the closest place to Christ is no guarantee of a true heart, and that even the betrayal of a chosen friend was woven into the redemption the whole chapter has been unfolding.

The witnesses also weigh the irony the Evangelist lets stand: "one of the twelve" — the very number Christ had named in the previous verse — is the betrayer, so that the chapter which most magnifies the security of those the Father gives (v.37, 39) ends by naming one who was near yet not given. Calvin and the grammatical commentators alike read it as the final guard against presumption: nearness to Christ, place among the chosen, even the confession of the lips, is no substitute for the heart the Father draws (v.44).
